subject: biology
greeting: Welcome! Glad you're grinding out some Biology revision. Quick heads-up — I'm Kairu, the Student Hub AI. I can make mistakes, so always cross-check tricky answers with your teacher or class notes. Now, let's get those marks!
note: Questions transcribed verbatim from real CSEC Biology Paper 1 past papers. Order, stems, and option positions preserved as in the original. Explanations and distractor analysis written by Kairu — Student Hub's AI tutor.

questions.stem: Which of the following features is used to classify a group of organisms as Class Insecta?
questions.explanation: Members of Class Insecta are defined by having a body divided into three segments — head, thorax and abdomen — together with three pairs of legs and (usually) one or two pairs of wings.

distractors.A: Insects vary hugely in size, from tiny aphids to large beetles. Size is not a classification feature.
distractors.B: Body shape varies (slender, round, flat). Not a classifying feature.
distractors.C: Insects come in every colour. Colour is for identification, not classification.

questions.stem: Living organisms, such as plants, are affected by ABIOTIC factors which determine where they become established. Which of the following lists contains some of these determining factors?

options.D: Deforestation, slash and burn, shifting cultivation

questions.explanation: Abiotic factors are the non-living physical and chemical components of an environment — light, temperature, water, pH, soil minerals, oxygen, etc. They determine which organisms can survive in a given habitat.

distractors.A: Sediment size can be abiotic, but shape and colour aren't usual determining factors.
distractors.C: Parasitism, commensalism and mutualism are biotic interactions between living organisms.
distractors.D: These are human activities that affect ecosystems — they aren't abiotic factors themselves.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly defines a niche and a habitat?

options.A: Niche: The role that an organism plays in the habitat in which it lives | Habitat: The place where an organism lives
options.B: Niche: The place where an organism lives | Habitat: The role that an organism plays in the habitat in which it lives
options.C: Niche: A community of living organisms that share the environment | Habitat: The abiotic and biotic factors of the ecosystem in which an organism lives
options.D: Niche: The biotic factors of the ecosystem where an organism lives | Habitat: The abiotic factors of the ecosystem where an organism lives

questions.explanation: The standard definitions: a niche is the role an organism plays in its habitat (what it eats, when it's active, how it interacts). A habitat is the place where it lives (forest floor, freshwater stream, coral reef).

distractors.B: Reverses the two terms.
distractors.C: Confuses these with community and ecosystem.
distractors.D: Niche is not just biotic factors and habitat is not just abiotic factors.

questions.stem: The following food chain shows the feeding relationship in a freshwater lake: Algae → larvae → eel → pike. Which organism in the food chain is the secondary consumer?
questions.explanation:
Working through the chain:

  • Algae = producer
  • Larvae = primary consumer (eats producer)
  • Eel = secondary consumer (eats primary consumer)
  • Pike = tertiary consumer

distractors.B: Pike eats the eel (secondary consumer), so pike is the tertiary consumer.
distractors.C: Algae makes its own food via photosynthesis — it's the producer.
distractors.D: Larvae eat algae directly, so they're the primary consumer.

questions.stem: Refer to the food chain Algae → larvae → eel → pike. Which of the following organisms in the food chain is the herbivore?
questions.explanation: A herbivore eats plants (or algae). The larvae feed directly on the algae (the producer), so the larvae are the herbivore.

distractors.A: Eel eats larvae (animals), so eel is a carnivore.
distractors.B: Pike eats eel (animals), so pike is a carnivore.
distractors.C: Algae is the producer — it doesn't eat anything; it makes its own food.

questions.stem: A farmer notices pink mealy bugs in his garden. He is advised to introduce the ladybird beetle to control the mealy bugs. The type of relationship between the mealy bug and the ladybird beetle is described as
questions.explanation: The ladybird beetle kills and eats the mealy bug. This is a clear predator–prey relationship — biological pest control works by exploiting it.

distractors.A: Commensalism: one benefits, the other is unaffected. Here the mealy bug is being killed, not unaffected.
distractors.C: Mutualism: both benefit. The mealy bug doesn't benefit from being eaten.
distractors.D: Parasitism: one (parasite) lives in/on a host and harms it slowly. Ladybirds kill quickly and don't live in/on the bug.

questions.stem: Approximately 10% of the energy stored in food is available to the next organism in a food chain because

options.A: most of the energy is lost during the process of excretion
options.B: there are fewer consumers than producers in a food chain
options.C: most of the energy is lost as heat during the process of respiration
options.D: many consumers compete for the same food source

questions.explanation: At each trophic level most of the energy taken in is used by the organism for its own metabolism. The bulk of this is released as heat during respiration, with smaller amounts lost in faeces and unconsumed parts. Only about 10% remains as biomass that the next consumer can eat.

distractors.A: Some energy is lost in excretion, but heat from respiration is the much bigger loss.
distractors.B: True statement, but it doesn't explain WHERE the energy goes.
distractors.D: Competition affects who gets food, not how much energy survives the transfer between trophic levels.

questions.stem: Animals that are at the end of a food chain and that feed on dead and decaying organisms are called
questions.explanation: Decomposers (and detritivores) feed on dead and decaying matter, returning nutrients to the soil for producers to use again.

distractors.A: Prey are animals that get eaten — they're at the start of a feeding relationship, not the end.
distractors.B: Omnivores eat both plants and animals (alive). Not specifically dead matter.
distractors.C: Carnivores eat live animal flesh — not dead and decaying organisms primarily.

questions.stem: The GREATEST threat to the survival of coral reefs is
questions.explanation: Rising ocean temperatures from climate change cause coral bleaching: corals expel the symbiotic algae (zooxanthellae) that give them food and colour, often leading to coral death. This is the single biggest threat to reefs worldwide.

distractors.A: Higher dissolved oxygen helps reef life, not harms it.
distractors.C: Decreased greenhouse gases would slow climate change — that's good for reefs.
distractors.D: Nitrogen pollution can cause problems, but decreased nitrogen alone isn't the greatest threat to reefs.

questions.stem: Replanting trees on a bare hillside is an example of
questions.explanation: Reafforestation = replanting trees in an area where forest existed previously but was cleared. Restoration = bringing a damaged ecosystem back toward its natural state. Both apply when replanting on a bare hillside that used to be forested.

distractors.A: Preservation = strict, hands-off protection of existing intact ecosystems. Replanting is active intervention, not preservation.
distractors.C: Afforestation is planting trees where there was no forest before — different from replanting on a bare hillside.
distractors.D: Neither preservation nor conservation involves the active replanting described here.

questions.stem: The graph below illustrates population growth. Phase 4 of the graph is MOST likely due to
questions.explanation: Phase 4 is the decline phase — population numbers are falling. Of the options listed, only competition from invasive species would push a population's numbers down. The others would maintain or increase the population.

distractors.A: Disease resistance helps a population survive — it would not cause decline.
distractors.B: A high birth rate increases the population. Decline comes from death rate exceeding birth rate.
distractors.C: Adequate food and space allows the population to stay at carrying capacity (phase 3), not crash.

diagram.alt: Population growth graph showing four phases — slow start, rapid rise, plateau, then decline

questions.stem: Which of the following structures is found ONLY in plant cells?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis. Only plants (and algae) have them, because only they make their own food from sunlight.

distractors.A: All eukaryotic cells (plant and animal) have a nucleus.
distractors.C: Both plant and animal cells respire aerobically, so both have mitochondria.
distractors.D: All cells have a cell membrane. Plants additionally have a cell wall outside it.

questions.stem: Which of the following comparisons between the cell wall and cell membrane is INCORRECT?

options.A: Cell Wall: Found in both plant and animal cells | Cell Membrane: Found in animal cells only
options.B: Cell Wall: Freely permeable | Cell Membrane: Differentially permeable
options.C: Cell Wall: Contains cellulose | Cell Membrane: Does not contain cellulose
options.D: Cell Wall: Found in plant cells only | Cell Membrane: Found in both plant and animal cells

questions.explanation: Statement A reverses the truth: cell walls are found only in plant cells, and cell membranes are found in both plant and animal cells. Every other comparison (B, C, D) is accurate.

distractors.B: This is correct — cell walls are freely permeable; cell membranes are partially/differentially permeable.
distractors.C: This is correct — plant cell walls are made of cellulose; cell membranes are made of phospholipids and proteins.
distractors.D: This is correct — and is the inverse of statement A.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a xylem vessel. The MAJOR feature that is responsible for the rigid structure of the xylem vessel is the
questions.explanation: Lignin is a strong, woody polymer deposited in the cell walls of xylem vessels. It is the substance that makes xylem rigid enough to provide mechanical support and prevent collapse under the negative pressure of water transport.

distractors.B: The cell wall provides structure, but it's the lignin within the wall that makes it rigid. Without lignin, the wall is not rigid.
distractors.C: The space inside the vessel is what water flows through — it doesn't provide rigidity.
distractors.D: Gaps allow water to move between cells; they reduce structural integrity rather than providing it.

diagram.alt: Xylem vessel labelled with space, cell wall containing lignin, and gap where two cells meet

questions.stem: In which of the following biological processes is diffusion involved? I. Food absorption II. Urine formation III. Gaseous exchange
questions.explanation:
Diffusion is involved in all three:

  • Food absorption — small soluble molecules diffuse from the gut lumen into intestinal villi.
  • Urine formation — selective reabsorption in the kidney tubules involves diffusion (alongside active transport and osmosis).
  • Gaseous exchange — O₂ and CO₂ move across the alveolar wall by diffusion.

distractors.A: Excludes gaseous exchange, which is a textbook example of diffusion.
distractors.B: Excludes urine formation, but reabsorption in the kidney does use diffusion.
distractors.C: Excludes food absorption, which uses diffusion for many small molecules.

questions.stem: The diagram below represents a metabolic process carried out in plants. The food produced is
questions.explanation:
Photosynthesis produces glucose, a simple sugar:

6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂

Plants later convert glucose into starch (storage), cellulose (structure), and other compounds.

distractors.A: Fats are made later from glucose and other intermediates — they aren't the direct product of photosynthesis.
distractors.B: Proteins require nitrogen and amino acids; they aren't made directly by photosynthesis.
distractors.D: Amino acids require nitrogen too — produced afterward, not by photosynthesis itself.

diagram.alt: Photosynthesis flow chart with water, light and carbon dioxide entering, oxygen and food leaving the plant cell containing chlorophyll in chloroplasts

questions.stem: The graph below shows the rate of photosynthesis versus light intensity at 0.03% CO₂. Which of the following statements BEST accounts for the shape of the graph?

options.A: As photosynthesis rates increase, there is a decrease in carbon dioxide levels.
options.B: As light intensity increases, the rate of photosynthesis also increases, until a stationary phase exists, when denaturation of the enzymes occurs.
options.C: As light intensity increases, there is an increase in photosynthesis, until there is no further increase in the rate due to some other limiting factor.
options.D: As carbon dioxide levels gradually increase, there is a similar increase in the rate of photosynthesis, until a plateau phase exists where there is no further increase in carbon dioxide and therefore no further increase in photosynthesis.

questions.explanation: The graph plots rate against light intensity (CO₂ is fixed at 0.03%). At first, more light → more photosynthesis. Eventually the curve plateaus because some other factor (CO₂ availability or temperature) becomes the limiting factor, so adding more light no longer helps.

distractors.A: The graph shows light intensity on the x-axis, not CO₂ levels.
distractors.B: Enzymes denature with high temperature, not high light intensity. Light intensity itself doesn't denature them.
distractors.D: This describes a different graph — one where CO₂ is on the x-axis. Here CO₂ is held constant at 0.03%.

diagram.alt: Graph showing rate of photosynthesis rising then plateauing as light intensity increases

questions.stem: Which of the following minerals is important for the formation of chlorophyll?
questions.explanation: Magnesium sits at the centre of every chlorophyll molecule. A magnesium-deficient plant develops yellow leaves (chlorosis) because it cannot make chlorophyll properly.

distractors.A: Iron helps in chlorophyll synthesis as a cofactor, but the actual atom at the centre of chlorophyll is magnesium.
distractors.B: Zinc is needed in trace amounts for some enzymes, not for chlorophyll itself.
distractors.C: Potassium is important for many plant processes but is not part of chlorophyll's structure.

questions.stem: An enzyme is BEST defined as a molecule which

options.A: increases the rate of a chemical reaction
options.B: decreases the rate of a chemical reaction
options.C: increases the rate of a chemical reaction but remains unchanged at the end of the reaction
options.D: decreases the rate of a chemical reaction but remains unchanged at the end of the reaction

questions.explanation: An enzyme is a biological catalyst: it speeds up a chemical reaction and is not consumed by the reaction, so it can be reused. Both halves of that definition matter.

distractors.A: True but incomplete — misses that enzymes are unchanged at the end (re-usable).
distractors.B: Enzymes accelerate reactions; they don't slow them down.
distractors.D: Enzymes increase, not decrease, reaction rate.

questions.stem: Which of the following graphs shows the effect of temperature on an enzyme-controlled reaction?
questions.explanation:
Enzymes follow a classic bell curve with temperature: activity rises as temperature increases (more kinetic energy → more successful collisions), peaks at the optimum temperature (~37 °C for most human enzymes), then drops sharply as the heat denatures the protein and destroys the active site.

Graph D matches this pattern.

distractors.A: Graph A shows activity decreasing steadily as temperature rises — but enzyme activity actually increases with temperature up to the optimum.
distractors.B: Graph B shows activity rising continuously up to 80 °C — but at very high temperatures enzymes denature, so the rate cannot keep climbing.
distractors.C: Graph C dips and then rises sharply — that's not the biological pattern. Enzyme activity has a single peak around the optimum, not a U-shape.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old male is advised by his doctor to reduce the amount of salt he consumes in his diet. If he ignores his doctor's advice, he has a high chance of developing
questions.explanation: High sodium (salt) intake raises blood pressure by causing water retention. Long-term high blood pressure = hypertension, a major risk factor for stroke and heart disease.

distractors.A: Obesity is linked more to total energy intake (calories) and physical inactivity than salt.
distractors.B: Diabetes is associated mainly with sugar intake and insulin resistance, not salt.
distractors.D: Sickle-cell anaemia is a genetic disorder you're born with — diet doesn't cause it.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the respiratory system with parts labelled I, II, III and IV. Which of the labelled parts represents the rib?
questions.explanation: Label II points to the rib — the bony structure forming part of the chest wall outside the lungs. I is the trachea, III is the bronchial tree inside the lung, IV is the diaphragm.

distractors.A: I is the trachea (windpipe) — the central vertical airway at the top.
distractors.C: III shows the bronchi/bronchioles inside the lung.
distractors.D: IV is the diaphragm — the dome-shaped muscle below the lungs.

diagram.alt: Respiratory system diagram with four parts labelled I (top vertical airway), II (chest wall side), III (lung tissue inner branches), IV (bottom dome-shaped structure)

questions.stem: The equation below shows the oxidation of glucose in cells: C₆H₁₂O₆ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O (release of energy). The energy released from the process above is

options.C: stored in a high-energy bond in ADP
options.D: stored in a high-energy bond in ATP

questions.explanation:
Energy from respiration is captured by phosphorylating ADP to make ATP:

ADP + Pi + energy → ATP

The energy is held in the high-energy phosphate bond at the end of ATP, where it can later be released for cellular work.

distractors.A: Energy isn't "converted to" ADP — ADP is the lower-energy form. We move from ADP to ATP.
distractors.B: Energy isn't converted to ATP itself; it is stored in the bond within ATP.
distractors.C: ADP is the lower-energy starting point. The high-energy bond is in ATP, not ADP.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT an effect of nicotine found in cigarette smoke?

options.C: Reduced oxygen transport by the blood
options.D: Increased mucous production in the cells lining the respiratory passage

questions.explanation: Nicotine actually paralyses the cilia lining the airways, decreasing their beating. Mucus and trapped particles can't be cleared, so they build up in the lungs (smoker's cough). The other three statements (A, C, D) are all genuine effects of smoking.

distractors.A: Nicotine is a stimulant — it raises heart rate. (This IS a real effect, so not the answer.)
distractors.C: Carbon monoxide in cigarette smoke binds haemoglobin and reduces oxygen transport. (Real effect.)
distractors.D: Smoke irritates the airways and increases mucus production. (Real effect.)

questions.stem: An amoeba obtains all the oxygen it needs by diffusion via its cell membrane, while a human needs to have special respiratory surfaces for this purpose. The BEST explanation for this difference is that

options.A: the amoeba does not require much oxygen
options.B: a human requires a larger volume of oxygen
options.C: oxygen cannot pass through the skin of a human
options.D: a human's surface area to volume ratio is too small for diffusion to be effective

questions.explanation: The key concept is surface area to volume ratio. As an organism gets bigger, its volume grows faster than its surface area, so simple diffusion across the body surface can't supply enough oxygen to the deeper tissues. Large organisms therefore evolved specialised respiratory surfaces (lungs, gills) with very large internal surface areas.

distractors.A: Oxygen need per cell is similar — the issue is geometry, not demand.
distractors.B: Larger volume of oxygen needed is a symptom of the underlying problem, but not the explanation.
distractors.C: Oxygen actually CAN diffuse through skin in small amounts (frogs do this), but it's not enough for a large mammal.

questions.stem: In heart bypass surgery, using an artery to bypass the blockage is advantageous over using a vein because

options.A: arteries carry blood away from the heart
options.B: veins allow blood to flow in one direction
options.C: arteries have thicker, more muscular walls than veins

questions.explanation: Arteries have thicker, more muscular walls built to handle the high pressure pumped from the heart. When grafted in a bypass, they hold up to that pressure better than veins, so they last longer and are less likely to fail.

distractors.A: True statement, but not the reason for choosing artery over vein in bypass surgery.
distractors.B: Veins do flow in one direction (using valves) — but that's true of arteries too in a sense, and isn't an advantage of veins for bypass.
distractors.D: Mostly true (the pulmonary vein is an exception), but it doesn't relate to durability of the graft.

questions.stem: The events listed below are involved in the clotting of blood: I. Fibrinogen is converted to fibrin. II. Blood cells are trapped. III. Platelets are activated. IV. Blood vessels are damaged. Which of the following is the correct sequence of events in the clotting of blood?
questions.explanation:
The clotting cascade in order:

  1. IV — vessel damaged (trigger).
  2. III — platelets activated at the wound site.
  3. I — fibrinogen converted to fibrin (long sticky threads).
  4. II — blood cells trapped in the fibrin mesh, forming the clot.

distractors.A: Starts with fibrin formation — but you need a vessel injury and platelets first.
distractors.B: Blood cells can't be trapped before fibrin is even made.
distractors.C: Platelets must be activated by an injury — you can't start the sequence with them.

questions.stem: Artificial immunity can BEST be described as immunity

options.A: acquired from the body's natural defense against disease
options.B: produced by deliberate exposure to a pathogen
options.C: that has been passed on from mother to child in the uterus
options.D: that has been passed on to a child from a mother's colostrum

questions.explanation: Artificial immunity means immunity that humans deliberately induce — most commonly through vaccination, where you are intentionally exposed to a weakened or killed pathogen so your body builds antibodies against it.

distractors.A: That's natural active immunity — your own body responds to a real infection.
distractors.C: That's natural passive immunity — antibodies cross the placenta from mother.
distractors.D: That's also natural passive immunity — antibodies in colostrum/breast milk.

questions.stem: Under which conditions will the rate of transpiration in plants be HIGHEST?
questions.explanation: Transpiration is fastest when stomata are wide open (light) AND water vapour leaves quickly from the leaf surface. Sun keeps stomata open and warms the leaf; high wind constantly removes humid air from the leaf surface, keeping the diffusion gradient steep. Therefore: sunny + high wind speed → maximum transpiration.

distractors.A: Sunny is good for transpiration, but low wind allows humid air to build up and slow it.
distractors.C: Cloudy weather generally means stomata partly closed — slower transpiration.
distractors.D: Wind helps, but cloudy weather still reduces stomatal opening compared to sunny.

questions.stem: In the transport of solutes in the phloem, the source is defined as any area of the plant where

options.A: water is loaded into the phloem
options.B: water is taken out of the phloem
options.C: sucrose is loaded into the phloem
options.D: sucrose is taken out of the phloem

questions.explanation: In phloem transport (translocation), a source is any place where sucrose is loaded INTO the phloem — typically the leaves, where it is made by photosynthesis. A sink is where sucrose is unloaded — fruits, growing tips, roots, storage organs.

distractors.A: Phloem transport is about sucrose movement; water moves passively along the gradient. The source isn't defined by water.
distractors.B: That happens at the sink, not the source — and again it's about sucrose, not water.
distractors.D: Sucrose being taken OUT defines the sink, not the source.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a potato plant with parts labelled I (leaves), II (stem at ground), III (potato tubers), IV (roots). Which part of the plant contains the MOST starch?
questions.explanation: The potato tuber (III) is a swollen underground storage organ specifically packed with starch — that's why potatoes are a staple carbohydrate food. Leaves and stems contain some starch but in much smaller amounts.

distractors.A: Leaves produce starch via photosynthesis but most is exported — not stored.
distractors.B: Stems transport substances; they aren't a major starch store.
distractors.D: Roots in a potato plant anchor and absorb water — they're not the main starch store; the tubers are.

diagram.alt: Potato plant diagram showing leaves above ground and tubers and roots below ground

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a form of excretion in plants?

options.A: Carbon dioxide diffusing in, through the stomata
options.B: Substances being stored in the bark of a tree
options.C: Water vapour diffusing out, through the epidermis
options.D: Calcium oxalate crystals being stored in leaves during leaf fall

questions.explanation: Excretion = removing waste FROM the body. CO₂ diffusing IN is the opposite — that's intake of CO₂ for photosynthesis. The other three describe genuine plant excretion: storing waste in dead tissues (bark), losing water vapour (transpiration), and dumping waste in leaves before they fall.

distractors.B: True excretion — plants store wastes in the dead bark which is shed.
distractors.C: True excretion — water vapour leaving the plant carries waste materials with it.
distractors.D: True excretion — calcium oxalate is a metabolic waste, dumped in leaves that drop.

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly describes movement in plants?
questions.explanation: Plant movements are typically growth-based (tropisms — directional growth toward stimuli like light or gravity) or part movements (e.g., the response of mimosa leaves). Tropic growth movements are irreversible — once a stem has grown toward light, it doesn't ungrow. Plants do not move as a whole organism (they're rooted).

distractors.A: Plants don't move as a whole organism — "whole" is wrong.
distractors.B: Same problem — plants don't move as a whole. And growth movements aren't reversible.
distractors.D: Growth itself is not reversible — once cells have elongated, they don't shrink back.

questions.stem: Locomotion is an evolutionary adaptation performed by animals. What advantages does this mechanism have over the type of movement that occurs in plants? I. The ability to move to favourable conditions II. The increased chance of predator attacks III. The ability to find food
questions.explanation: I and III are real advantages — locomotion lets animals seek favourable conditions and find food. II is a disadvantage, not an advantage: moving makes you more visible to predators. Be careful — the question asks for advantages only.

distractors.A: Includes II (predator attacks), which is a disadvantage of locomotion.
distractors.C: Includes II and excludes the real advantage of moving to favourable conditions.
distractors.D: Lumps in II — but the question asked for advantages only.

questions.stem: The diagrams below illustrate the effects of light on a shoot under different conditions: I (intact tip, light from one side), II (tip removed, light from one side), III (foil cap on tip, light from one side), IV (transparent cap on tip, light from one side). After a few days, the TALLEST shoot would MOST likely be
questions.explanation: Tip-removed shoots (II) cannot grow at all — auxin comes from the tip. Shoots I and IV both detect light through the tip, redistribute auxin, and bend toward the light (so vertical height is reduced). Shoot III has a foil cap blocking light from reaching the tip, so no phototropism occurs — auxin is produced and distributed evenly, and the shoot grows straight upward, ending up the tallest in vertical height.

distractors.A: I has its tip exposed and bends toward the light — vertical height reduced.
distractors.B: II has no tip = no growth at all. Shortest, not tallest.
distractors.D: IV has a transparent cap — light gets through, tip senses it, shoot bends. Shorter than III in vertical height.

diagram.alt: Four shoots in light. I has an intact tip; II has its tip removed; III has a foil (opaque) cap; IV has a transparent cap

questions.stem: The role of an effector is to
questions.explanation: An effector is a muscle or gland that responds to a nerve signal — for example, by contracting (muscle) or secreting (gland). It carries out the response.

distractors.A: Detection is the job of a receptor, not an effector.
distractors.C: Relay is what relay (interneurone) neurones in the CNS do.
distractors.D: Nerve impulses are created by neurones in response to receptor input — not by the effector.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a spinal reflex involving skin of the hand, biceps muscle, and the spinal cord. Three neurones are labelled I (between biceps and cord), II (between skin and cord), III (inside the cord). Which of the following options gives the correct names for the numbered structures?

options.A: I sensory; II motor; III relay
options.B: I sensory; II relay; III motor
options.C: I motor; II sensory; III relay
options.D: I motor; II relay; III sensory

questions.explanation:
A reflex arc runs: receptor (skin) → sensory neurone → relay neurone (in spinal cord) → motor neurone → effector (muscle).

Mapping to the diagram: I connects spinal cord to biceps muscle = motor. II connects skin to spinal cord = sensory. III is inside the cord = relay.

distractors.A: Reverses sensory and motor positions.
distractors.B: Has II as relay, but II is the neurone outside the cord (carrying signal in from skin) — that's sensory.
distractors.D: Has III as sensory — but III is inside the cord, so it's the relay neurone.

diagram.alt: Spinal reflex diagram showing skin of hand, biceps muscle and spinal cord, with three neurones labelled I, II and III

questions.stem: Which of the following are functions of the cerebellum in the brain? I. Maintains posture II. Maintains balance III. Regulates temperature
questions.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates muscle movement, posture, and balance. Temperature regulation is the job of the hypothalamus, not the cerebellum, so III is excluded.

distractors.B: III is wrong — temperature is hypothalamus, not cerebellum.
distractors.C: I is genuinely a cerebellum function — leaving it out is incorrect.
distractors.D: Includes III, which is hypothalamus, not cerebellum.

questions.stem: Which of the following factors does NOT contribute to the development of glaucoma in the eye?
questions.explanation: Glaucoma is caused by raised pressure inside the eye damaging the optic nerve. Risk factors include age, family history (heredity) and certain medical conditions. Vitamin A deficiency causes a different set of eye problems — night blindness and xerophthalmia — not glaucoma.

distractors.A: Age is a major glaucoma risk factor (incidence rises sharply over 60).
distractors.B: Stroke can affect the eye through optic nerve damage and is associated with glaucoma in some cases.
distractors.C: Family history substantially raises glaucoma risk — heredity is a known factor.

questions.stem: The graph below illustrates a measurement of growth in living organisms (sigmoid curve). Which of the following labels would be INCORRECT on the y-axis?
questions.explanation: Time is the independent variable in a growth curve — it goes on the x-axis. The y-axis must be the dependent variable: a measure of growth itself, like mass, length, or count.

distractors.A: Mass is a valid measure of growth — fine on the y-axis.
distractors.C: Length (height) is a valid measure of growth — fine on the y-axis.
distractors.D: Counting leaves is a valid measure of plant growth — fine on the y-axis.

diagram.alt: Sigmoid (S-shaped) growth curve

questions.stem: The following activities are involved in the menstrual cycle: I. Repair of the uterine lining II. Ovulation III. Shedding of the uterine lining IV. Development of the Graafian follicle. Which of the following sequences represents the order of activities in the menstrual cycle?
questions.explanation:
Starting from early in the cycle:

  1. IV — Graafian follicle develops (follicular phase, around days 1–13)
  2. I — Repair of the uterine lining (endometrium thickens, days ~5–14)
  3. II — Ovulation (around day 14)
  4. III — Shedding of the lining (menstruation, if no fertilisation)

distractors.A: Has shedding before Graafian follicle development — out of order.
distractors.B: Starts with ovulation, but follicle development must come first.
distractors.D: Reverses the cycle entirely — shedding doesn't come immediately after follicle development.

questions.stem: The production of new organisms from one parent only is known as
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction involves only one parent and produces offspring genetically identical to the parent. Examples: binary fission, budding, vegetative propagation.

distractors.A: Mitosis is a type of cell division used during asexual reproduction, but it's not the term for producing new organisms.
distractors.B: Meiosis produces sex cells (gametes) for sexual reproduction, requiring two parents.
distractors.C: Sexual reproduction needs two parents who contribute gametes.

questions.stem: The diagrams below show two specialized cells: a large round cell with a nucleus (egg/ovum) and a smaller cell with a long tail (sperm). Which of the following structures is common to both cells?
questions.explanation: Both gametes carry a nucleus containing genetic material — that is in fact the whole point of fertilisation: combining the nuclei of egg and sperm. The other structures listed are sperm-specific.

distractors.A: The acrosome (cap of digestive enzymes) is found only on the sperm head.
distractors.B: Flagellum (tail) is found only on the sperm.
distractors.D: Cilia are short hair-like structures, not typical of either gamete; egg cells have neither flagella nor cilia.

diagram.alt: Egg cell and sperm cell shown side by side

questions.stem: Which row in the following table CORRECTLY states the cause, treatment, control and prevention of the disease AIDS?

options.B: RNA virus / Antiretrovirals, for example, AZT / Monogamy / Abstinence
options.C: DNA virus / Antigens / Vaccination / Public education

questions.explanation: AIDS is caused by HIV, an RNA retrovirus. It is treated with antiretroviral drugs like AZT (treatment, not cure). Spread is controlled by reducing risky behaviour (e.g., monogamy, condom use), and prevention starts with abstinence.

distractors.A: HIV is a virus, not a bacterium. Antibiotics don't work on viruses.
distractors.C: HIV is RNA, not DNA, and "antigens" aren't a treatment. There is currently no widely used preventive vaccine.
distractors.D: HIV isn't a bacterium and penicillin has no effect on viruses.

questions.stem: The diagrams below show flowers from two different types of plants. Flower I has bright tubular petals; Flower II has feathery stigmas and dangling stamens with no obvious petals. Which of the following statements is true for the flowers?

options.A: I is pollinated by a hummingbird; II is pollinated by the wind.
options.C: Petals are absent from both I and II.

questions.explanation: Flower I has showy, tubular petals typical of bird-pollinated flowers (often red, with nectar deep in the tube). Flower II has feathery stigmas and exposed dangling stamens typical of wind-pollinated flowers (no need for showy petals).

distractors.B: II has features of wind pollination, not bird pollination.
distractors.C: Flower I clearly shows petals — they aren't absent from both.
distractors.D: Flower I is showy and tubular — typical of animal pollination, not wind.

diagram.alt: Two flowers — flower I with showy tubular petals and flower II with feathery stigma and dangling stamens

questions.stem: Which of the following shows the correct sequence for seed formation after fertilization?
questions.explanation:
The ovule already exists before fertilisation. After fertilisation:

  1. Ovule contains the egg.
  2. Zygote forms when sperm and egg fuse.
  3. Embryo develops as the zygote divides.
  4. Seed matures (the ovule, now containing the embryo, develops a seed coat).

distractors.A: Has zygote at the end — but the zygote forms BEFORE the embryo.
distractors.C: Has embryo first, but the embryo can't exist before the zygote.
distractors.D: Same problem — embryo can't come before zygote.

questions.stem: A vector is defined as an organism that
questions.explanation: A vector is a living organism (commonly an arthropod like a mosquito or tick) that transmits a pathogen from one host to another. It is the courier, not the cause.

distractors.A: Some vectors bite, but the definition isn't about biting — it's about transmission.
distractors.B: The actual pathogen causes the disease. The vector merely carries it.
distractors.C: That describes parasites or symbionts, not vectors specifically.

questions.stem: Which of the following forms of birth control is MOST likely to be 100% effective?
questions.explanation: Tubal ligation is a surgical procedure that cuts and seals the fallopian tubes, preventing the egg from reaching the uterus. It's effectively permanent and over 99% effective — closer to 100% than any of the other options.

distractors.A: Condoms are about 85% effective with typical use, much less than tubal ligation.
distractors.B: Diaphragms are about 88% effective with typical use.
distractors.D: The pill is about 91% effective with typical use (missed doses, drug interactions reduce reliability).

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man has been diagnosed with hypertension as well as diabetes. Which of the following treatments would be MOST effective for the diseases?

options.A: Exercising and lowering the intake of salt and refined carbohydrates
options.B: Spending more time watching television to relax

questions.explanation: Lifestyle modification simultaneously tackles both: less salt reduces blood pressure (hypertension), fewer refined carbohydrates reduces blood-sugar spikes (diabetes), and regular exercise improves both. This is the standard first-line treatment.

distractors.B: More TV is the opposite of helpful — sedentary behaviour worsens both conditions.
distractors.C: "Rigorous" exercise can be unsafe for an untrained 60-year-old with these conditions, and it doesn't address diet.
distractors.D: Insulin only helps diabetes (mainly Type 1) and does nothing for hypertension.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST describes a chromosome?

options.A: Two forms of the same gene
options.B: Structure made up of DNA wrapped around histones
options.C: Nucleic acid that contains all genetic information
options.D: Part of the DNA which carries genetic information to produce a protein

questions.explanation: A chromosome is the visible packaged form of DNA — DNA wrapped around histone proteins, condensed during cell division. This is the textbook structural definition.

distractors.A: Two forms of the same gene are alleles, not chromosomes.
distractors.C: Nucleic acid containing all genetic information describes the genome (or DNA itself), not a single chromosome.
distractors.D: That's the definition of a gene — a section of DNA coding for a protein.

questions.stem: Which of the following are examples of asexual reproduction? I. Budding II. Binary fission III. Vegetative propagation
questions.explanation:
All three are classic forms of asexual reproduction:

  • Budding — new individual grows out of the parent (e.g., yeast, hydra).
  • Binary fission — single cell splits into two (e.g., bacteria, amoeba).
  • Vegetative propagation — new plants grow from runners, tubers, cuttings, etc.

distractors.A: Vegetative propagation is also asexual — leaving it out is incorrect.
distractors.B: Binary fission is also asexual — leaving it out is incorrect.
distractors.C: Budding is also asexual — leaving it out is incorrect.

questions.stem: On a certain Caribbean island where sugar cane is grown, a fungus is accidentally introduced into the island and destroys the entire sugar cane crop. Which of the following BEST explains why the entire crop was destroyed by the fungus?

options.A: The plants were old and therefore more susceptible to the fungus.
options.B: The plants were not of a hardy enough variety to withstand the fungus.
options.C: The plants were planted too close together so the fungus spread easily.
options.D: There was no genetic variation among the plants so none could survive the attack.

questions.explanation: Sugar cane is grown as a monoculture — all plants are clones, genetically identical. Because they share the same genome, they share the same susceptibilities. A fungus that can defeat one plant's defences can defeat them all. The lesson: lack of genetic diversity makes monocultures vulnerable to wipe-outs.

distractors.A: Age might affect a few plants, but not explain destruction of the entire crop uniformly.
distractors.B: "Not hardy enough" is vague and doesn't explain the universal vulnerability.
distractors.C: Spacing affects spread speed, but if there were genetic resistance some plants would survive.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about meiosis is NOT true?

options.B: It results in the production of gametes.
options.C: It doubles the number of chromosomes in gametes.
options.D: It causes haploid cells to form from diploid cells.

questions.explanation: Meiosis HALVES, not doubles, the chromosome number. A diploid cell (2n) goes through two divisions to produce four haploid cells (n) — each with half the parent's chromosomes. The doubling happens at fertilisation when sperm and egg fuse.

distractors.A: True — crossing over and independent assortment in meiosis create genetic variation.
distractors.B: True — meiosis produces gametes (sperm and egg cells).
distractors.D: True — meiosis converts diploid cells into haploid cells.

questions.stem: If a diploid organism has two different alleles for the same gene, it is described as being
questions.explanation: Heterozygous means having two different alleles for a gene (e.g., Tt). Homozygous means two identical alleles (TT or tt). Dominant and recessive describe how alleles behave, not whether two alleles match.

distractors.B: Homozygous would mean two identical alleles, the opposite of what the question describes.
distractors.C: "Dominant" describes an allele's expression, not the genotype configuration.
distractors.D: "Recessive" likewise describes an allele's expression.

questions.stem: Albinism is caused by a recessive allele. Two normal parents produce an albino child. This is because

options.B: both parents were heterozygous for the gene
options.C: one parent was homozygous dominant for the trait and the other heterozygous
options.D: one parent was homozygous dominant for the trait and the other homozygous recessive

questions.explanation: For an albino child to be born (genotype aa), both parents must each carry at least one recessive allele. Since both parents are normal in appearance, neither can be aa — they must each be heterozygous (Aa) carriers. The Punnett: Aa × Aa → 1AA : 2Aa : 1aa, giving a 25% chance of an albino child.

distractors.A: Both homozygous recessive parents (aa × aa) would themselves be albino — but the question says they are normal.
distractors.C: Homozygous dominant × heterozygous (AA × Aa) cannot produce an aa child.
distractors.D: Homozygous dominant × homozygous recessive (AA × aa) gives all Aa offspring — none would be albino.

questions.stem: Two goats, heterozygous for fast growth rate, are crossed. What percentage of the entire F1 population would be expected to possess homozygous alleles?
questions.explanation:
Cross: Aa × Aa

Punnett square gives the genotype ratio 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa. The homozygous offspring are AA (25%) and aa (25%) — together 50%.

distractors.A: 25% would be the homozygous DOMINANT (AA) only, or homozygous RECESSIVE (aa) only — but the question asks for ALL homozygous combined.
distractors.B: 75% is the proportion that show the dominant phenotype (AA + Aa), not the proportion that are homozygous.
distractors.D: 100% would mean all offspring homozygous, which isn't possible from a heterozygous × heterozygous cross.

questions.stem: Variation in a population is due to I. mutation II. crossing over III. asexual reproduction
questions.explanation: Mutation (random changes in DNA) and crossing over during meiosis both produce genetic variation. Asexual reproduction produces clones — offspring genetically identical to the parent — so it does NOT add new variation (except via the rare mutation).

distractors.B: Asexual reproduction does not produce variation, so III shouldn't be included.
distractors.C: Mutation IS a source of variation — leaving it out is wrong.
distractors.D: III (asexual reproduction) doesn't produce variation, so it shouldn't be included.

questions.stem: As a result of mitosis each daughter cell has

options.B: twice the number of chromosomes as the parent cell
options.C: the same number of chromosomes as the parent cell
options.D: half the number of chromosomes as the parent cell

questions.explanation: Mitosis is a single division that produces two genetically identical daughter cells, each with the same chromosome number as the parent. It is used for growth and repair, not for making gametes.

distractors.A: Mitosis produces a fixed, exact copy — the chromosome number is not variable.
distractors.B: Doubling happens during S phase (DNA replication) but the cell then divides, halving back to the parent's number.
distractors.D: Halving is what meiosis does, not mitosis.

questions.stem: Which of the following descriptions is true of natural and artificial selection?

options.A: Natural Selection: Occurs in domestic populations | Artificial Selection: Occurs in natural populations
options.B: Natural Selection: Involves genetic modification | Artificial Selection: Largely controlled by the environment
options.C: Natural Selection: Produces great biological diversity | Artificial Selection: Produces very different organisms from natural populations
options.D: Natural Selection: Is a faster process | Artificial Selection: Is a slower process

questions.explanation: Natural selection acts on wild populations over very long periods, producing the rich biological diversity we see in nature. Artificial selection (selective breeding by humans) targets specific traits and can produce organisms that look very different from their wild relatives — think Chihuahua vs wolf, or modern corn vs teosinte.

distractors.A: Reverses the two — natural selection occurs in natural populations; artificial selection occurs in domestic populations.
distractors.B: Reverses again — natural selection is largely controlled by the environment; artificial selection involves human-directed selection (and increasingly genetic modification).
distractors.D: Reverses speed — natural selection is generally slower; artificial selection can produce changes within a few generations.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows Stages I, II, III and IV in the production of insulin by genetic engineering. Stage I shows the plasmid extracted and cut open; Stage II shows the insulin gene cut from human DNA; Stage III shows the recombinant plasmid; Stage IV shows the bacterium containing copies of the recombinant plasmid. In which of the stages above can insulin be produced by the bacterium?
questions.explanation: Insulin is produced when the genetically modified bacterium expresses the inserted human insulin gene. This happens at Stage IV, where the bacterium contains the recombinant plasmid and reproduces, mass-producing insulin.

distractors.A: At stage I the plasmid has just been extracted and opened — no insulin gene is in it yet.
distractors.B: At stage II the insulin gene has just been cut from human DNA — it isn't in any bacterium yet.
distractors.C: At stage III the recombinant plasmid exists but hasn't yet been put into a host bacterium and replicated.

diagram.alt: Genetic engineering of insulin: human cell with chromosomes, bacterium with plasmids, gene extraction, plasmid cutting, recombinant plasmid, and bacterium reproducing with multiple recombinant plasmids

questions.stem: Which of the following characteristics is used to distinguish insects from other Arthropods?
questions.explanation: Insects always have three pairs (six) of legs. Other arthropod classes differ: arachnids have 8 legs, crustaceans have 10 or more, and myriapods have many. The leg count is the most reliable distinguishing feature.

distractors.B: Some insects (bees, moths) are hairy; others (beetles) are not. Hair is not a reliable class-level feature.
distractors.C: Colour varies enormously within Insecta and overlaps with other arthropods.
distractors.D: Insect body shapes vary widely (long, round, flat) — not a defining feature.

questions.stem: Living organisms, such as plants, are affected by ABIOTIC factors which determine where they become established. Which of the following lists describes some of these determining factors?

options.D: Deforestation, slash and burn, shifting cultivation

questions.explanation: Abiotic factors are the non-living physical and chemical components of an environment — light, water, temperature, pH, oxygen, soil minerals. They determine which organisms can survive in a given habitat.

distractors.A: These are biotic interactions between living organisms.
distractors.C: Sediment size is a physical factor, but shape and colour are not standard abiotic determinants.
distractors.D: These are human activities that affect ecosystems — they aren't abiotic factors themselves.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows energy transfer between the sun, producers, consumers and decomposers (with heat lost at each step). Energy through ecosystems is not 100% efficient. This is so because energy is

options.B: recycled from plants to the atmosphere
options.C: circular, moving in and out of organisms
options.D: linear, moving from one organism to the next

questions.explanation: At every trophic level, organisms use most of the energy they consume for their own metabolism. The bulk is released as heat during respiration, with smaller amounts lost in faeces and excretion. Only ~10% remains as biomass for the next consumer.

distractors.B: Energy is not recycled — only nutrients are. Energy flows one way through ecosystems.
distractors.C: Energy flow is one-way (linear), not circular.
distractors.D: This describes the linear nature of energy flow but doesn't explain why it's not 100% efficient.

diagram.alt: Energy flow diagram with sun, producers, consumers, decomposers and inorganic nutrient pool

questions.stem: On which trophic level can herbivores MOST likely be found?
questions.explanation: Herbivores are primary consumers — they eat producers. The trophic levels go: 1st = producers, 2nd = primary consumers (herbivores), 3rd = secondary consumers (carnivores eating herbivores), 4th = tertiary consumers.

distractors.A: The first trophic level is occupied by producers (plants), not consumers.
distractors.C: The third trophic level is for secondary consumers — carnivores that eat herbivores.
distractors.D: Tertiary consumers occupy the fourth level.

questions.stem: Commensalism is illustrated by the relationship between a
questions.explanation: The cattle egret follows grazing cows and eats insects disturbed by their movement. The egret benefits; the cow is unaffected. That's the textbook definition of commensalism.

distractors.A: Dog and flea is parasitism — the flea benefits while the dog is harmed.
distractors.B: Hen and chick is a parental relationship within the same species, not commensalism.
distractors.D: Man and mosquito is parasitism — mosquito benefits, man is harmed (and may get disease).

questions.stem: The diagram shows a food web with Hawk, Ocelot, Squirrel, Iguana and Plants. How many complete food chains can be made from the food web shown?
questions.explanation:
Each chain must start with the producer (Plants) and end with a top consumer (Hawk or Ocelot). Tracing every path:

  1. Plants → Squirrel → Hawk
  2. Plants → Squirrel → Ocelot
  3. Plants → Iguana → Ocelot

Three complete chains in total.

distractors.A: There is more than one path — Squirrel feeds two predators and Iguana feeds Ocelot.
distractors.B: Two chains misses one of the three paths through the web.
distractors.D: Four would require a fourth path — e.g. Plants → Iguana → Hawk — but Hawk does not eat Iguana in this web.

diagram.alt: Food web with Hawk, Ocelot, Squirrel, Iguana and Plants

questions.stem: Which of the following is the MAIN advantage of recycling?
questions.explanation: The primary environmental and economic benefit of recycling is that it reduces the demand for virgin raw materials — saving forests, mineral deposits, and the energy needed to extract and process new resources.

distractors.A: Recycled products are not necessarily more durable than new ones.
distractors.C: Cheaper price is sometimes a benefit but not the main advantage of recycling overall.
distractors.D: Recycling does create jobs, but that's a side benefit — the main advantage is conservation of raw materials.

questions.stem: 50 g of fresh sample of soil is repeatedly heated until 110 °C and cooled in a desiccator. The final constant weight of the soil is 35 g. The component MOST likely eliminated by this procedure is
questions.explanation: Heating to 110 °C drives off water (boiling point 100 °C). The 15 g loss represents the soil's water content. Burning off humus would require much higher temperatures (~500 °C+).

distractors.A: Air is already eliminated quickly at low temperatures, but it doesn't account for a 15 g weight loss — air is too light.
distractors.C: Humus only burns off above 500 °C — well above the 110 °C used here.
distractors.D: Minerals don't decompose at 110 °C; they require furnace temperatures to break down.

questions.stem: Which of the following are effects of pollutants on coral reefs in the Caribbean? I. Increase in macroalgal and seagrass growth II. Loss of reef fish III. More branching corals
questions.explanation: Nutrient pollution causes macroalgae and seagrass to bloom, smothering corals (I). Pollution also kills fish through low oxygen and toxicity (II). However, branching corals are more sensitive to pollution and DECREASE — III is wrong.

distractors.B: III is wrong: branching corals don't increase, they decline.
distractors.C: Excludes I (algal overgrowth), which is a real effect.
distractors.D: Includes III, which is incorrect — branching corals are reduced, not increased.

questions.stem: Which of the following practices are used in the conservation or restoration of an ecosystem? I. Restricting hunting seasons II. Planting of mangroves along the shoreline III. Quarrying to remove limestone
questions.explanation: Restricting hunting (I) protects animal populations. Planting mangroves (II) restores coastal ecosystems and prevents erosion. Quarrying (III) destroys ecosystems — the opposite of conservation.

distractors.A: II is also a real conservation practice — leaving it out is wrong.
distractors.B: I is also a real conservation practice — leaving it out is wrong.
distractors.D: III (quarrying) is destructive, not conservation.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows human population size over a period of time, with peaks and troughs from 1971 to 1997. Which of the following factors MOST likely account for the change in population size from January 1980 to January 1989? I. Increase in contraceptive use II. Influenza disease III. Decrease in fetal census
questions.explanation: The two main drivers of population change are birth rate and death rate. Increased contraceptive use (I) reduces birth rate; influenza outbreaks (II) increase death rate. Both directly affect population size. "Decrease in fetal census" (III) is not a standard CSEC factor and would simply reflect fewer births rather than cause them.

distractors.B: III is not a recognised driver of population change.
distractors.C: Excludes I (contraceptive use), which is a major real factor.
distractors.D: Includes III, which isn't a standard population factor.

diagram.alt: Graph of human population size over time from 1971 to 1997

questions.stem: Which of the following organelles is directly involved in photosynthesis?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are the site of photosynthesis. Light is absorbed by chlorophyll inside the chloroplast, where the carbohydrate (glucose) is then made.

distractors.A: Mitochondria are the site of cellular respiration, not photosynthesis.
distractors.C: The cytoplasm is the gel-like fluid in the cell — it doesn't carry out photosynthesis.
distractors.D: The nucleus stores DNA but doesn't perform photosynthesis.

questions.stem: Which of the following structures are found in BOTH the generalised plant cell and the animal cell?

options.B: Cell wall, cell membrane and nucleus

questions.explanation: Both plant and animal cells have cell membranes, mitochondria and vacuoles (small ones in animals; one large central one in plants). Cell walls and chloroplasts are found in plants only.

distractors.A: Cell wall is plant-only — not in animal cells.
distractors.B: Cell wall is plant-only.
distractors.C: Chloroplast is plant-only — not in animal cells.

questions.stem: The role of respiration is the
questions.explanation: Respiration's primary role is to release the chemical energy stored in glucose (and other foods) so it can be used for the cell's activities. Oxygen uptake, CO₂ release and the breakdown of glucose are all steps in achieving that.

distractors.B: Absorption of oxygen happens during aerobic respiration but it's a means, not the role.
distractors.C: Releasing CO₂ is a by-product of respiration, not its role.
distractors.D: Breaking down carbohydrates is a step within respiration, but the role is to RELEASE the energy from that breakdown.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows two solutions of different concentrations of sugar separated by a membrane, with a water level rising. Which of the following correctly identifies and describes the process occurring above?

options.A: Diffusion — Water moving from concentrated to dilute solution
options.B: Osmosis — Water moving from dilute to concentrated solution
options.C: Diffusion — Sugar moving from dilute to concentrated solution
options.D: Osmosis — Sugar moving from concentrated to dilute solution

questions.explanation: Osmosis is the movement of water across a partially permeable membrane from a region of HIGHER water concentration (dilute) to LOWER water concentration (concentrated). The water level rises on the concentrated side as water moves in.

distractors.A: Water doesn't flow from concentrated to dilute — that's the wrong direction. And this is osmosis, not diffusion.
distractors.C: Sugar molecules are too large to cross the partially permeable membrane easily; this is water movement, not sugar.
distractors.D: Osmosis specifically refers to water movement, not sugar.

diagram.alt: Osmosis demonstration with concentrated sugar solution, dilute sugar solution, separated by a partially permeable membrane

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST describes the nucleus of a cell?

options.D: It stores genetic information in the form of DNA.

questions.explanation: The nucleus is the cell's control centre. It contains chromosomes made of DNA, which carry the genetic instructions for everything the cell does.

distractors.A: The nuclear membrane has pores — it is selectively permeable.
distractors.B: Starch is stored in chloroplasts (in plants), not the nucleus.
distractors.C: Mitochondria are in the cytoplasm, outside the nucleus.

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT necessary for photosynthesis to take place?
questions.explanation:
Oxygen is a product of photosynthesis, not a requirement. The reactants are water and CO₂, energy comes from light, and chlorophyll is the catalyst:

6CO₂ + 6H₂O → C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6O₂ (with light + chlorophyll)

distractors.A: Water is one of the reactants — needed.
distractors.B: Light provides the energy — needed.
distractors.D: Chlorophyll absorbs the light — essential.

questions.stem: After absorption by the ileum, excess glucose is immediately converted to

options.B: glycogen by the liver and muscles
options.C: fat in the liver and muscles
options.D: fat by the liver and stored under the skin

questions.explanation: Excess blood glucose is taken up by the liver and muscles and converted to glycogen for short-term storage. When blood glucose drops, glycogen is broken back down to release glucose.

distractors.A: Some glucose is used for immediate energy via respiration, but the EXCESS is stored.
distractors.C: Long-term excess (when glycogen stores are full) is converted to fat — but the FIRST step is glycogen.
distractors.D: Storage as fat under the skin happens only after glycogen stores are saturated.

questions.stem: Which of the following is optimum for the action of salivary amylase? (rows show Temperature, pH, Amount of Maltose Produced)
questions.explanation: Salivary amylase works in the mouth, so its optimum is body temperature (~37 °C, in the 30–40 range) and near-neutral pH (around 7–8). Row B has both — and the highest maltose yield (73 mg) confirms it.

distractors.A: Temperature too low and pH too acidic — amylase wouldn't work well in the stomach's acid environment.
distractors.C: Temperature is right but pH 9–10 is too alkaline — amylase activity drops outside its optimum pH.
distractors.D: Temperature 40–50 °C is too high — the enzyme starts to denature.

questions.stem: An athlete suffered muscle cramps following his race. The muscle cramps are MOST likely caused by an accumulation of
questions.explanation: During hard exercise, oxygen supply to muscles can't keep up with demand. Muscles switch to anaerobic respiration, producing lactic acid as a waste product. Lactic acid build-up causes the burning sensation and cramps.

distractors.A: Urea is produced by the liver from amino acids — not from muscle activity.
distractors.B: Oxygen is consumed during exercise, not accumulated.
distractors.D: Glucose is consumed during exercise — its accumulation isn't what causes cramps.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite illustrates an investigation on a well-watered, de-starched plant which was left for a few hours in sunlight, with part of a leaf covered by foil. After testing for starch with iodine: the covered area was white, the uncovered area was blue-black. A likely explanation of this result is that the

options.A: covered part of the leaf died
options.B: soil around the plant dried out
options.C: foil prevented light from entering the leaf
options.D: foil prevented carbon dioxide from entering the leaf

questions.explanation: The foil blocked light. Without light, photosynthesis can't occur, so no starch is made — that's why the covered area gave the negative iodine test (white). The uncovered area got light and made starch (blue-black).

distractors.A: The leaf isn't dead — it just lacks starch in the covered region.
distractors.B: The plant was well-watered, so this is ruled out by the experimental setup.
distractors.D: Foil doesn't really block CO₂ — gases can leak around the edges. The key variable being controlled here is light.

diagram.alt: Plant leaf with foil covering part of it, demonstrating starch production needs light

questions.stem: Which feature correctly distinguishes a phagocyte from a lymphocyte?

options.A: Phagocyte engulfs pathogens (Yes); Lymphocyte engulfs pathogens (No)
options.B: Phagocyte produces antibodies (Yes); Lymphocyte produces antibodies (No)
options.C: Phagocyte has a lobed nucleus (No); Lymphocyte has a lobed nucleus (Yes)
options.D: Phagocyte formed in bone marrow (Yes); Lymphocyte formed in bone marrow (Yes)

questions.explanation: Phagocytes (e.g., neutrophils) engulf and digest pathogens — that's their job. Lymphocytes don't engulf — they make antibodies (B-cells) or directly kill infected cells (T-cells). So 'engulfing pathogens' cleanly distinguishes the two.

distractors.B: REVERSED — lymphocytes (B-cells specifically) produce antibodies, not phagocytes.
distractors.C: REVERSED — phagocytes have lobed (multi-lobed) nuclei; lymphocytes have a single round nucleus.
distractors.D: BOTH are formed in bone marrow — so this doesn't distinguish them.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a section through an incisor tooth, with regions labelled I (top), II, III and IV (bottom near jawbone). The region of the tooth sensitive to temperature is labelled
questions.explanation: The pulp cavity in the centre of the tooth contains nerves and blood vessels. It's the only part that can detect temperature, pressure and pain. Label III in this diagram corresponds to the pulp.

distractors.A: I is enamel — the hardest, outermost layer. It has no nerves and is insensitive.
distractors.B: II is dentine — has some sensitivity but the main pain/temperature receptors are in the pulp.
distractors.D: IV is part of the root or jawbone — outside the pulp.

diagram.alt: Section through an incisor tooth labelled I, II, III, IV with jawbone and gum visible

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows the respiratory system with parts labelled I, II, III and IV. Which of the labelled parts represents the rib?
questions.explanation: The ribs form the bony cage around the lungs. In standard CSEC diagrams, the rib is shown along the chest wall outside the lungs — corresponding to label II here.

distractors.A: I is the trachea — the windpipe at the top.
distractors.C: III is the bronchial tree inside the lung.
distractors.D: IV is the diaphragm at the bottom.

diagram.alt: Respiratory system labelled I (top airway), II (chest wall), III (inside lung), IV (bottom muscle)

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT correct for the process of inhalation?
questions.explanation: During inhalation, the ribs move UP and OUT (driven by intercostal muscle contraction), the diaphragm flattens downward, and these together increase the thoracic cavity volume — drawing air in. Option A reverses the rib direction.

distractors.B: True — air does move IN during inhalation.
distractors.C: True — the diaphragm contracts and flattens during inhalation.
distractors.D: True — increased thoracic volume reduces internal pressure, drawing air in.

questions.stem: Which of the following options BEST identifies some of the transport substances in animals? I. Amino acids II. Hormones III. Sucrose
questions.explanation: Animals transport amino acids in blood (I) and hormones via the endocrine system (II). Sucrose is transported in PLANTS (in the phloem), not in animals. Animals transport glucose, not sucrose.

distractors.B: Includes sucrose — wrong for animals.
distractors.C: Excludes amino acids (transported in blood) and includes sucrose (a plant transport sugar).
distractors.D: Includes sucrose — wrong for animals.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows the human circulatory system with vessels labelled I (between heart and lungs), II (between liver and small intestines), III (next to kidneys). Which of the following correctly identifies the vessels labelled I, II and III?

options.A: I = Pulmonary artery; II = Hepatic artery; III = Mesenteric vein
options.B: I = Pulmonary vein; II = Hepatic portal vein; III = Renal artery
options.C: I = Aorta; II = Renal artery; III = Pulmonary artery
options.D: I = Pulmonary artery; II = Hepatic portal vein; III = Renal vein

questions.explanation: I is the pulmonary vein (lungs → heart, oxygenated blood — veins always return blood to the heart). II is the hepatic portal vein (small intestines → liver, carrying digested nutrients). III is the renal artery (carrying blood from the aorta to the kidneys for filtration).

distractors.A: Hepatic artery and mesenteric vein are real vessels but don't match the directions in this diagram.
distractors.C: Aorta carries blood from heart to body, not specifically heart to lungs.
distractors.D: I shows blood flowing lungs → heart in the diagram, so it must be a vein, not the pulmonary artery.

diagram.alt: Human circulatory system showing lungs, heart, liver, small intestines, kidneys, legs with three labelled vessels

questions.stem: A person whose kidneys have failed must undergo a process by which excretory materials are removed from the blood regularly. This is because excretory materials

options.C: are not gotten rid of by any other means
options.D: would otherwise accumulate and poison the person

questions.explanation: Urea and other nitrogenous wastes are toxic in high concentrations. Without kidney filtration (or dialysis to substitute), they accumulate in the blood and cause uraemia, which is fatal if untreated.

distractors.A: Some excretory products affect blood pressure, but this isn't why dialysis is necessary.
distractors.B: Dialysis isn't about diluting blood — it's about removing wastes.
distractors.C: Some excretion happens through other routes (skin, lungs) but they can't replace the kidneys for nitrogenous waste.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a section of the heart, with sections I and II labelled. In a patient with a certain defective heart condition, blood flowed from Section I to Section II in the wrong direction. This was MOST likely due to malfunction of the
questions.explanation: Backflow between the heart's chambers is prevented by atrioventricular valves. The bicuspid (mitral) valve sits between the left atrium and left ventricle. If it fails, blood can leak backward — the most common cause of section-to-section reverse flow in the left heart.

distractors.A: The atrium is a chamber, not a valve. A malfunctioning chamber wouldn't cause backflow specifically.
distractors.B: Same — the ventricle is a chamber. The valve is what controls direction.
distractors.C: Semi-lunar valves sit between the ventricles and the great arteries (aorta and pulmonary artery), not between heart sections.

diagram.alt: Section of the human heart with two regions labelled I and II

questions.stem: The following statements describe some of the stages of the clotting of blood: I. Platelets are activated. II. Thrombin converts fibrinogen to fibrin. III. Prothrombin is converted to thrombin. IV. A clot is formed. Which of the following correctly identifies the sequence of events in the process of clotting?
questions.explanation:
The clotting cascade in correct order:

  1. I — Platelets activate at the wound, releasing clotting factors.
  2. III — Prothrombin → thrombin (catalysed by released factors).
  3. II — Thrombin converts fibrinogen → fibrin (the sticky mesh).
  4. IV — Clot forms as red cells get trapped in the fibrin mesh.

distractors.B: Has fibrinogen→fibrin before prothrombin→thrombin — but you need thrombin first to do that conversion.
distractors.C: Starts with fibrinogen→fibrin — needs platelets activated first to start the cascade.
distractors.D: Starts with prothrombin→thrombin — needs platelet activation first to release the factors that trigger that conversion.

questions.stem: Why is it difficult to develop a vaccine for the common cold?

options.A: The antibodies for the common cold are difficult to produce.
options.B: The antigens on the common cold virus remain the same.
options.C: The antigens on the common cold virus change frequently.
options.D: Human beings cannot produce antibodies for the common cold.

questions.explanation: Common cold viruses (rhinoviruses, plus dozens of strains) mutate frequently. The antibodies your body made against last year's strain don't recognise this year's. This is also why we need annual flu shots — for the same reason.

distractors.A: Antibodies against cold viruses are produced normally — that's not the obstacle.
distractors.B: REVERSED — the antigens change, they don't stay the same.
distractors.D: Humans DO produce antibodies against cold viruses (that's how we recover from each cold). They just don't help against new strains.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a cactus with thorns (reduced leaves), modified stem and deep penetration root system. Which of the following describes the function of the thorns?
questions.explanation: Cactus thorns are modified leaves. By being needle-thin (instead of broad and flat), they have a tiny surface area — so very little water is lost by transpiration. This is critical for survival in dry environments.

distractors.A: Thorns aren't involved in seed/fruit dispersal.
distractors.B: Thorns DECREASE surface area — that's the whole point. More surface = more water loss.
distractors.D: This is a secondary effect — thorns may deter herbivores, but the primary evolutionary function is water conservation.

diagram.alt: Cactus showing thorns (reduced leaves), modified stem and deep root system

questions.stem: Which of the following rows correctly shows the metabolic substances that are excreted by plants and animals? (Plants | Animals)
questions.explanation: Plants excrete calcium oxalate (often stored in leaves, then dropped) and tannins. Animals excrete water (urine, sweat), urea and CO₂. Row D matches: calcium oxalate is plant excretion, water is animal excretion.

distractors.A: Urea is animal excretion; CO₂ is excreted by both, but is mostly thought of as animal output.
distractors.B: Calcium oxalate is plant excretion, not animal — wrong column.
distractors.C: Oxygen is a plant byproduct of photosynthesis, not an animal excretion.

questions.stem: Which of the following is LEAST likely to be an example of a growth movement in a plant?

options.A: The closing of the leaves of a sensitive plant
options.B: The downward movement of the roots in the soil
options.C: The crawling movement of runners like grasses
options.D: The upward movement of a vine on a tall pole

questions.explanation: The closing of mimosa (sensitive plant) leaves is a nastic movement — caused by rapid changes in cell turgor, NOT by growth. The other three are tropisms (growth-based: roots = positive geotropism, runners = lateral growth, vine = positive thigmotropism).

distractors.B: Roots grow downward toward gravity — that's geotropic growth, irreversible.
distractors.C: Runners grow horizontally — that's also growth, irreversible.
distractors.D: A vine climbing is positive thigmotropism — growth in response to touch.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about the skeletal system is NOT correct?

options.A: It protects delicate organs such as the heart and lungs.
options.B: It is made up of hard non-living tissues.
options.C: It produces red and white blood cells.
options.D: It gives the body its shape.

questions.explanation: Bone IS hard, but the tissue is very much alive. It contains living osteocytes, blood vessels, nerves, and bone marrow. The hardness comes from the calcium-phosphate matrix, but the cells producing and maintaining that matrix are living.

distractors.A: True — the rib cage protects the heart and lungs; the skull protects the brain.
distractors.C: True — red and white blood cells are made in the bone marrow.
distractors.D: True — without the skeleton, the body would have no defined shape.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows muscles X and Y attached to the bones of the arm. Which of the following occurs when the arm is flexed?

options.A: Muscle X Contracts; Muscle Y Relaxes
options.B: Muscle X Contracts; Muscle Y Contracts
options.C: Muscle X Relaxes; Muscle Y Relaxes
options.D: Muscle X Relaxes; Muscle Y Contracts

questions.explanation: The biceps and triceps are an antagonistic pair. When the arm bends (flexes), the biceps (Y) contracts to pull the forearm up, and the triceps (X) relaxes to allow that movement.

distractors.A: REVERSED — that's the pattern for arm extension (straightening), not flexion.
distractors.B: Both muscles contracting at once would lock the arm — antagonistic muscles work in opposition.
distractors.C: Both relaxing means no movement at all.

diagram.alt: Diagram showing arm flexion with muscles X and Y attached to scapula, origin and insertion

questions.stem: A detectable change in the internal or external environment of an organism is called
questions.explanation: A stimulus is the change that triggers a reaction. A receptor detects the stimulus, the response is the reaction, and the effector carries out the response.

distractors.B: A response is what the organism DOES in reaction to the stimulus.
distractors.C: A receptor is the structure that detects the stimulus (e.g., taste buds, eyes).
distractors.D: An effector is the muscle or gland that carries out the response.

questions.stem: A girl smells a hamburger that is being cooked by her mother and develops a watery mouth. Which of the following is the effector which brings about her response?
questions.explanation: The effector is the structure that carries out the response. In this case, the salivary glands are the effector — they produce saliva (the response) when triggered by the smell signal.

distractors.A: Cells in the nose are the RECEPTORS (they detect the smell), not the effector.
distractors.C: The smell of food is the STIMULUS, not an effector.
distractors.D: Secretion of saliva is the RESPONSE — what the effector does, not the effector itself.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows an eye's response to looking at an object: pupils get smaller (Before → After). The response is MOST likely brought about by

options.A: an increase in the light intensity
options.B: a decrease in the light intensity
options.D: the object being moved further away

questions.explanation: The pupillary light reflex: bright light → pupils constrict (get smaller) to limit the amount of light reaching the retina and protect it from damage. Dim light → pupils dilate.

distractors.B: Less light would cause pupils to DILATE (get larger), not constrict.
distractors.C: Looking at a close object does involve some pupil constriction, but the primary trigger for this reflex is light intensity.
distractors.D: Object moving further away wouldn't change pupil size in this way.

diagram.alt: Eye showing larger pupils Before and smaller pupils After

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a section of the skin with regions labelled I, II, III and IV. The region which acts in a similar manner to SPF (sun protection factor) creams is
questions.explanation: The Malpighian layer (stratum basale) at label III contains melanocytes that produce melanin. Melanin absorbs UV radiation and protects the deeper skin tissues from damage — exactly what SPF creams are designed to do. People with more melanin are naturally more protected from UV.

distractors.A: I is the outer/cornified layer of dead cells — no melanin production happens here.
