distractors.B: II is upper epidermis above the melanin-producing layer.
distractors.D: IV is the dermis/subcutaneous tissue (blood vessels, sweat glands) — no UV protection role.

diagram.alt: Section through human skin labelled I, II, III, IV from surface to deep

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows an eye with near-sightedness (myopia). Which of the following shows how the defect can be corrected? (Lens / Bending of Light Rays Before Entering the Eye)
questions.explanation: Myopia (near-sightedness) means light focuses BEFORE the retina because the eyeball is too long or the lens too convex. A diverging (concave) lens spreads the light rays OUTWARDS before they enter the eye, shifting the focal point back onto the retina.

distractors.B: Diverging lenses spread light outwards, not inwards.
distractors.C: Converging lenses are used to correct hyperopia (long-sightedness), not myopia.
distractors.D: Converging lenses bend light inwards, but that's the wrong correction for myopia.

diagram.alt: Eye showing near-sightedness (myopia) with light focusing before the retina

questions.stem: The following statements describe the processes taking place within a seed during germination. I. Embryo uses food to develop radicle and plumule. II. Enzymes break down proteins into amino acids. III. Soluble products move into the embryo. Which of the following correctly identifies the sequence of events during germination of the seed?
questions.explanation:
Germination order:

  1. II — Enzymes break down stored food (proteins → amino acids, starches → sugars).
  2. III — Soluble products move from the cotyledon/endosperm to the embryo.
  3. I — Embryo uses the food to develop the radicle (first root) and plumule (first shoot).

distractors.A: Has the embryo using food before the food has been broken down — out of order.
distractors.B: Same problem — embryo using food first.
distractors.D: Has the embryo using food before soluble products have moved to it.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite illustrates a measurement of growth in living organisms (sigmoid curve, growth on y-axis, time on x-axis). Which of the following options would be INCORRECT on the y-axis?
questions.explanation: Time is the independent variable on a growth curve — it goes on the x-axis. The y-axis shows the dependent variable: a measure of growth (mass, length, count).

distractors.A: Mass is a valid growth measure for the y-axis.
distractors.B: Length (height) is a valid growth measure for the y-axis.
distractors.D: Number of leaves is a valid growth measure for the y-axis (especially in plants).

diagram.alt: Sigmoid (S-shaped) growth curve with time on x-axis

questions.stem: The diagrams opposite show flowers from two different types of plants. Which of the following statements is TRUE for both flowers?

options.B: Petals are absent from both I and II.
options.C: Both are pollinated by a hummingbird.
options.D: I is pollinated by a hummingbird; II is pollinated by the wind.

questions.explanation: Flower I has bright tubular petals — typical of bird-pollinated flowers (hummingbirds love tubular shapes with nectar deep inside). Flower II has feathery stigmas and dangling stamens — classic features of wind-pollinated flowers.

distractors.A: Flower I is showy and tubular — that's bird pollination, not wind.
distractors.B: Flower I clearly has petals.
distractors.C: Flower II has features adapted for wind, not for hummingbirds.

diagram.alt: Two flowers — I bright tubular petals (bird-pollinated), II feathery stigmas and dangling stamens (wind-pollinated)

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a fetus in the uterus with parts labelled I, II, III and IV. The amnion is labelled
questions.explanation: The amnion is the inner membrane surrounding the fetus and enclosing the amniotic fluid. In this diagram, label IV points to the amniotic membrane wrapping the fetus.

distractors.A: I is the uterine wall.
distractors.B: II is the placenta.
distractors.C: III is the umbilical cord.

diagram.alt: Fetus in the uterus with four labels I, II, III, IV

questions.stem: Refer to the same fetal diagram. The placenta is labelled
questions.explanation: The placenta is the disc-shaped organ embedded in the uterine wall — it's where maternal and fetal blood exchange materials. In this diagram, label II points to the placenta.

distractors.A: I is the uterine wall surrounding the placenta.
distractors.C: III is the amnion (membrane around the fetus).
distractors.D: IV is typically the umbilical cord or amniotic fluid.

diagram.alt: Same fetus in uterus diagram with parts labelled I-IV

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a consequence of a plant or human disease?

options.D: Larger part of national budget used to buy medications

questions.explanation: Disease typically REDUCES production (less crop yield, more sick workers), causing food prices to RISE (less supply, same demand). A decrease in food prices is the OPPOSITE of what disease causes.

distractors.B: True — sick workers and damaged crops mean lower productivity.
distractors.C: True — sick students miss school.
distractors.D: True — governments must spend more on medicines and treatment during outbreaks.

questions.stem: One method of getting rid of mosquitoes is by getting rid of all stagnant water. Which stages of the life cycle does this method control?
questions.explanation: Mosquitoes lay eggs on water, the larvae develop in water, and the pupae also live in water. Removing stagnant water kills all three of these aquatic stages. Adult mosquitoes fly and aren't directly affected by water removal.

distractors.A: Adults fly — water removal doesn't reach them directly.
distractors.C: Adults aren't aquatic; eggs are missing from this option.
distractors.D: Larvae are missing — they're highly vulnerable in water.

questions.stem: Production of new organisms from one parent only is known as
questions.explanation: Asexual reproduction involves only one parent and produces offspring genetically identical to the parent. Examples include binary fission, budding, and vegetative propagation.

distractors.A: Sexual reproduction needs two parents who contribute gametes.
distractors.C: Mitosis is a type of cell division — used during asexual reproduction but isn't itself the term for the production of new organisms.
distractors.D: Meiosis produces gametes for sexual reproduction.

questions.stem: Two alleles of a gene are situated at

options.A: the same locus on a homologous pair of chromosomes
options.B: different loci on a homologous pair of chromosomes
options.C: the same locus on a different homologous pair of chromosomes
options.D: different loci on a different homologous pair of chromosomes

questions.explanation: Alleles of a gene occupy the same position (locus) on the two chromosomes of a homologous pair. One allele on each chromosome of the pair — that's why offspring inherit two copies of every gene.

distractors.B: Different loci would mean different genes, not different alleles.
distractors.C: Different homologous pairs would mean unrelated chromosomes — the alleles wouldn't be of the same gene.
distractors.D: Same problem — different chromosomes mean different genes.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows a section of a pair of homologous chromosomes that codes for hair colour, with one chromosome carrying Allele A and the other carrying Allele a. Which of the following describes the genotype shown?
questions.explanation: Heterozygous means having two different alleles for a gene (Aa). Homozygous would mean two identical alleles — either AA (dominant) or aa (recessive).

distractors.A: Homozygous would be AA or aa — both alleles the same. This shows A and a, which are different.
distractors.C: Homozygous recessive is aa — both lowercase. Not what's shown.
distractors.D: Homozygous dominant is AA — both uppercase. Not what's shown.

diagram.alt: Pair of homologous chromosomes with Allele A on one and Allele a on the other

questions.stem: Two goats, heterozygous for fast growth rate, are crossed. The percentage of the F1 population which possesses homozygous alleles is
questions.explanation: Cross: Aa × Aa. Punnett gives 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa. The homozygous offspring are AA (25%) and aa (25%) — combined 50% homozygous.

distractors.A: 25% would be only homozygous DOMINANT (AA) or only homozygous RECESSIVE (aa) — but the question asks for ALL homozygous combined.
distractors.B: 75% is the proportion showing the dominant phenotype (AA + Aa), not the homozygous proportion.
distractors.D: 100% homozygous is impossible from a heterozygous × heterozygous cross — half the offspring will be Aa (heterozygous).

questions.stem: Albinism is caused by a single recessive allele. Two normal parents have an albino child. This is because

options.A: both parents were heterozygous for the gene
options.B: both parents were homozygous recessive for the gene
options.C: one parent was heterozygous dominant for the trait and the other heterozygous
options.D: one parent was homozygous dominant for the trait and the other homozygous recessive

questions.explanation: For an albino child (genotype aa) to be born, BOTH parents must contribute a recessive 'a' allele. Since both parents are normal (not albino), they can't be aa themselves — they must each be heterozygous (Aa) carriers. The cross Aa × Aa gives 25% chance of an aa child.

distractors.B: Homozygous recessive (aa) parents would themselves be albino — but the question says they are normal.
distractors.C: "Homozygous dominant for the trait" would be AA — and AA × Aa cannot produce an aa child.
distractors.D: AA × aa gives all Aa offspring — none would be albino.

questions.stem: Which of the following options correctly describes DNA, chromosome, gene and allele? (DNA / Chromosome / Gene / Allele)

options.A: Deoxyribonucleic acid / DNA + histamine / Unit that codes for a specific protein / An alternate form of a gene
options.B: Unit that codes for a specific protein / An alternate form of a gene / DNA + protein / Hizones
options.C: Nucleic acid that has all genetic information / DNA + histones / The smallest unit of inheritance / An alternate form of a gene
options.D: Nucleic acid that has all genetic information / inheritance / The smallest unit of specific protein / DNA + protein

questions.explanation:
The textbook definitions:

  • DNA — nucleic acid carrying genetic information.
  • Chromosome — DNA wrapped around histone proteins.
  • Gene — smallest unit of inheritance (a section of DNA coding for one trait/protein).
  • Allele — alternate form of a gene.

Row C matches all four.

distractors.A: "Histamine" is wrong — chromosomes wrap around histones, not histamine (an inflammatory chemical).
distractors.B: Reverses several definitions and includes "Hizones" which isn't a real term.
distractors.D: Mismatches the gene definition and uses garbled wording.

questions.stem: The diagram opposite shows the production of insulin by genetic engineering, with stages I (gene cut from human DNA), II (placed in bacterial plasmid?), III (recombinant plasmid back in bacterium), IV (bacterium reproducing). At which of the stages shown in the diagram can insulin be produced by the bacterium?
questions.explanation: Insulin is produced when the genetically modified bacterium expresses the inserted human insulin gene. This happens at stage IV, where the bacterium contains the recombinant plasmid and reproduces, mass-producing insulin.

distractors.A: At stage I the gene has just been cut from human DNA — not yet in any bacterium.
distractors.B: At stage II the gene is being inserted into the plasmid — no production yet.
distractors.C: At stage III the recombinant plasmid is just back in a bacterium — production happens once the bacterium grows and expresses the gene (stage IV).

diagram.alt: Genetic engineering diagram showing human cell with chromosome, bacterium with plasmid, and four stages of insulin production

questions.stem: Which of the following sampling methods is MOST suitable for estimating the density of mushrooms in a garden?
questions.explanation: A quadrat is a frame of known area placed at random over the population. Counts within the quadrat are used to estimate density. It works for stationary organisms like mushrooms, plants, and barnacles.

distractors.B: Line transects sample along a single line — better for showing distribution change with environmental gradient than density.
distractors.C: Belt transects sample within a strip — also better for distribution patterns.
distractors.D: Mark-release-recapture is for mobile animals (fish, birds), not stationary mushrooms.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a marine food web (Large fish, Marine mammals, Small fish and crustaceans, Zooplankton, Phytoplankton). Trophic level 1 is represented by
questions.explanation: Trophic level 1 is always the producers. In aquatic ecosystems, the producers are the phytoplankton — microscopic photosynthetic organisms that form the base of the food web.

distractors.A: Zooplankton are primary consumers (eat phytoplankton) — trophic level 2.
distractors.C: Marine mammals are top consumers — trophic level 3 or 4.
distractors.D: Small fish and crustaceans eat zooplankton — trophic level 3.

diagram.alt: Marine food web with phytoplankton, zooplankton, small fish and crustaceans, marine mammals, large fish

questions.stem: Which of the following organisms are MOST important in biodegradation?
questions.explanation: Bacteria (and fungi) are the primary decomposers. They secrete enzymes that break down organic matter into simpler compounds, returning nutrients to the soil. They drive biodegradation at the chemical level.

distractors.B: Vultures are scavengers — they eat dead flesh but don't biodegrade waste at the molecular level.
distractors.C: Viruses are parasites that infect cells — they don't decompose dead material.
distractors.D: Earthworms break down organic matter mechanically and improve soil but the chemical decomposition is done by bacteria.

questions.stem: Which of the following feeding relationships may present harm to one of the organisms involved? I. Commensalism II. Parasitism III. Predation
questions.explanation: Parasitism harms the host. Predation harms (kills) the prey. Commensalism involves no harm — one benefits, the other is unaffected.

distractors.A: Commensalism doesn't harm anyone.
distractors.B: Commensalism doesn't harm anyone.
distractors.D: Includes commensalism, which is harmless to both.

questions.stem: Approximately 10% of the energy stored in food is used by the next organism in a food chain because

options.A: most of the energy is lost as heat during the process of respiration
options.B: most of the energy is lost during the process of respiration
options.C: there are fewer consumers than producers in a food chain
options.D: many consumers compete for the same food source

questions.explanation: At each trophic level, organisms use most of the energy they consume for their own metabolism. The bulk is released as heat during respiration, with smaller amounts lost in faeces and unconsumed parts. Only ~10% remains as biomass for the next consumer.

distractors.B: Similar but missing 'as heat' — the more precise answer specifies heat loss.
distractors.C: True statement, but it doesn't explain WHERE the energy goes.
distractors.D: Competition affects who gets food, not how much energy survives the transfer.

questions.stem: Which of the following materials are considered to be non-biodegradable?
questions.explanation: Sardine cans are made of metal — they don't break down by microbial action. They corrode over decades but they aren't biodegraded by living organisms.

distractors.A: Grass cuttings are organic and decompose quickly — biodegradable.
distractors.C: Newspapers are paper (cellulose) — biodegradable.
distractors.D: Most cloths (cotton, wool) are biodegradable, though synthetic fibres are less so.

questions.stem: Human activities would have the LEAST immediate impact on
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels form over millions of years from compressed organic remains. Human activities can't accelerate this process — it's on a geological timescale, not an immediate one.

distractors.A: Eutrophication can happen quickly when fertilisers run off into water — immediate impact.
distractors.B: Overpopulation responds within human lifetimes to birth rates and policies — immediate.
distractors.C: Coral bleaching has been visibly accelerating over decades due to warming — immediate impact.

questions.stem: The graph below shows human population size over a period of time, with peaks and troughs from 1971 to 2007. Which of the following factors MOST likely account for the change in population size from 1980 to 1989? I. Increase in contraceptive use II. Epidemic of a deadly disease III. Decrease in fatal crimes
questions.explanation: Two main drivers of population change: birth rate and death rate. Increased contraceptive use (I) reduces birth rate; an epidemic (II) increases death rate. Both directly affect population. "Decrease in fatal crimes" (III) is not a standard population factor.

distractors.B: III is not a recognised driver of population change.
distractors.C: Excludes I, which is a major real factor.
distractors.D: Includes III, which isn't a standard factor.

diagram.alt: Population graph 1971-2007

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a cell with structures labelled I, II, III and IV. Which of the labelled structures is responsible for controlling cellular activities?
questions.explanation: The nucleus is the cell's control centre — it contains DNA which directs every cellular activity. In typical CSEC cell diagrams, label II points to the nucleus.

distractors.A: I is usually the cell membrane (the boundary).
distractors.C: III is often the cytoplasm.
distractors.D: IV is typically a cell wall, mitochondrion, or vacuole — not the control centre.

diagram.alt: Cell with four labelled structures

questions.stem: Which of the following comparisons of the cell wall and cell membrane is INCORRECT?

options.A: Cell Wall: Found in both plant and animal cells | Cell Membrane: Found in animal cells only
options.B: Cell Wall: Freely permeable | Cell Membrane: Differentially permeable
options.C: Cell Wall: Contains cellulose | Cell Membrane: Does not contain cellulose
options.D: Cell Wall: Found in plant cells only | Cell Membrane: Found in both plant and animal cells

questions.explanation: Statement A reverses the truth: cell walls are found only in plant cells, and cell membranes are found in both plant and animal cells. Every other comparison (B, C, D) is accurate.

distractors.B: This is correct — cell walls are freely permeable; cell membranes are partially permeable.
distractors.C: This is correct — plant cell walls are made of cellulose.
distractors.D: This is correct — and is the inverse of statement A.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a potato in a petri dish with distilled water. What can be done to cause the level of solution in the petri dish to rise after 20 minutes?

options.A: Add 5 g of salt to the distilled water in the potato only
options.B: Add 5 g of salt to the distilled water in the petri dish only
options.C: Add 5 g of salt to the distilled water in both the petri dish and the potato
options.D: Add 0 g of salt to the distilled water in both the petri dish and the potato

questions.explanation: Adding salt to the petri dish makes that solution hypertonic compared to the potato cells. Water moves OUT of the potato and INTO the petri dish by osmosis — raising the level in the dish.

distractors.A: Salt in the potato makes the potato hypertonic — water moves IN to the potato, lowering the dish level.
distractors.C: Salt in both keeps concentrations equal — no osmotic gradient, no level change.
distractors.D: No salt added means no change in conditions — level stays the same.

diagram.alt: Potato in petri dish with distilled water

questions.stem: Autotrophic organisms derive their nutrition from
questions.explanation: Autotrophs (e.g., plants) make their own food from simple inorganic substances — carbon dioxide, water, and minerals — using energy from sunlight (in photosynthesis).

distractors.A: Water is needed but isn't the only inorganic input — CO₂ and minerals are also required.
distractors.B: Heterotrophs (animals) consume organic substances; autotrophs make their own.
distractors.D: Autotrophs use only inorganic substances; if they needed organic ones, they'd be heterotrophs.

questions.stem: Which of the following features does NOT enhance a leaf's ability to absorb solar energy?

options.A: Numerous chloroplasts in the palisade cells

questions.explanation: The vascular bundle contains xylem (water transport) and phloem (sugar transport). Its function is transport, not light absorption. The other three features all directly enhance light capture.

distractors.A: More chloroplasts = more chlorophyll = more light absorbed.
distractors.C: A transparent cuticle lets light pass through to reach the chloroplasts.
distractors.D: A large surface area exposes more cells to incoming sunlight.

questions.stem: The following food test results were obtained on samples X, Y and Z: X gives violet colour with NaOH and copper sulphate; Y gives blue/black with iodine; Z gives orange precipitate with Benedict's reagent ONLY after hydrolysis. Samples X, Y and Z most likely contain

options.A: X: non-reducing sugar | Y: starch | Z: protein
options.B: X: protein | Y: reducing sugar | Z: starch
options.C: X: starch | Y: protein | Z: reducing sugar
options.D: X: protein | Y: starch | Z: non-reducing sugar

questions.explanation:
Reading each test:

  • X — Biuret (NaOH + CuSO₄) → violet = protein
  • Y — iodine → blue-black = starch
  • Z — Benedict's reagent ONLY after hydrolysis → must be a non-reducing sugar (e.g., sucrose). Hydrolysis breaks it into reducing sugars which then give the positive Benedict's test.

distractors.A: X (Biuret positive) is protein, not non-reducing sugar.
distractors.B: Y (iodine positive) is starch, not reducing sugar.
distractors.C: Y (iodine positive) is starch — and Z (orange precipitate after hydrolysis) is non-reducing sugar.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a longitudinal section of a tooth, with regions labelled I, II, III and IV. Which region of the tooth contains a large number of blood vessels and nerves?
questions.explanation: The pulp cavity in the centre of the tooth contains nerves and blood vessels. It's the only part that can detect temperature, pressure, and pain. Label III in this diagram corresponds to the pulp.

distractors.A: I is enamel — the hardest, outermost layer with no nerves.
distractors.B: II is dentine — has some sensitivity but the main pain/temperature receptors are in the pulp.
distractors.D: IV is part of the root — outside the pulp.

diagram.alt: Longitudinal section of tooth

questions.stem: A 50-year-old male is advised by his doctor to pay special attention to the amount of salt in his diet. He probably has a high chance of developing
questions.explanation: High sodium (salt) intake raises blood pressure by causing water retention. Long-term high blood pressure = hypertension, a major risk factor for stroke and heart disease.

distractors.A: Obesity is linked more to total calories than salt.
distractors.B: Diabetes is associated mainly with sugar intake.
distractors.D: Sickle-cell anaemia is genetic — diet doesn't cause it.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a respiratory structure with red blood cells flowing through a U-shaped channel. The respiratory structure shown above is that of
questions.explanation: This diagram shows a single alveolus — the U-shape is the cup-shaped alveolar sac with capillaries (containing the labelled red blood cell) wrapping around its outside. Gas exchange happens across the thin alveolar wall: oxygen diffuses into the blood and carbon dioxide diffuses out.

distractors.B: The trachea is a wide tube that carries air to the bronchi — it's not a site of gas exchange and doesn't have capillaries wrapping around it.
distractors.C: A bronchiole is a small branching airway leading to the alveoli — also not a site of gas exchange.
distractors.D: A gill filament has a different shape — flat plates with secondary lamellae and water flowing OUTSIDE in countercurrent — not the cupped sac shown here.

diagram.alt: U-shaped respiratory structure with red blood cell

questions.stem: Which of the following is an effect of nicotine in cigarette smoke that is NOT TRUE?

options.C: Reduced oxygen transport by the blood
options.D: Increased mucous production in the cells lining the respiratory passage

questions.explanation: Nicotine actually paralyses the cilia lining the airways, decreasing their beating. Mucus and trapped particles can't be cleared, so they build up (smoker's cough). The other three are real effects.

distractors.A: Nicotine is a stimulant — it raises heart rate. (Real effect.)
distractors.C: Carbon monoxide in cigarette smoke binds haemoglobin and reduces oxygen transport. (Real effect.)
distractors.D: Smoke irritates airways, increasing mucus production. (Real effect.)

questions.stem: The essential vitamin and mineral ion required for blood clotting in humans are
questions.explanation: Blood clotting requires vitamin K (needed to synthesise clotting factors in the liver) and calcium ions (Ca²⁺) (essential cofactor in the clotting cascade).

distractors.A: Vitamin C is for collagen synthesis, not directly clotting. K⁺ (potassium) is for nerve/muscle function.
distractors.B: Vitamin B is for metabolism. Na⁺ is for osmotic balance.
distractors.C: Vitamin A is for vision. Mg²⁺ is for enzymes/bone, not specifically clotting.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a mammalian kidney nephron with parts labelled I, II, III. Which of the following correctly identifies the functioning of EACH of the labelled parts I, II and III?

options.A: I: Ultrafiltration | II: Selective reabsorption | III: Water conservation
options.B: I: Reversible | II: Selective reabsorption | III: Water conservation
options.C: I: Water conservation | II: Under the influence of ADH | III: Urine production
options.D: I: Selective reabsorption | II: Ultrafiltration | III: Under the influence of ADH

questions.explanation:
Reading the diagram from top to bottom — I labels the Bowman's capsule / glomerulus, II labels the proximal / distal convoluted tubule, and III labels the loop of Henle.

  • I (Bowman's capsule / glomerulus) — ultrafiltration (filters blood under pressure to produce filtrate).
  • II (PCT / DCT) — selective reabsorption of useful substances (glucose, amino acids, salts, water) back into the blood.
  • III (loop of Henle) — water conservation (sets up the concentration gradient in the medulla so water can be reabsorbed in the collecting duct).

distractors.B: Misnames I — "Reversible" isn't a nephron function. The convoluted tubules and the loop are also swapped relative to the diagram.
distractors.C: Mixes up nephron processes — "under the influence of ADH" describes the collecting duct, which isn't labelled here, and "urine production" isn't a labelled stage.
distractors.D: Swaps ultrafiltration (I) with selective reabsorption (II / III). The first thing that happens in the nephron is filtration at the glomerulus, not reabsorption.

diagram.alt: Mammalian kidney nephron with three labelled parts

questions.stem: Locomotion is an evolutionary adaptation performed by animals. What advantages does this mechanism have over the type of movement that occurs in plants? I. The ability to move to favourable conditions II. Increases chances of predator attacks III. The ability to find food
questions.explanation: I and III are real advantages — locomotion lets animals seek favourable conditions and find food. II is a disadvantage: moving makes you more visible to predators.

distractors.A: Includes II (predator attacks), which is a disadvantage.
distractors.C: Includes II and excludes the real advantage of moving to favourable conditions.
distractors.D: Lumps in II — but the question asked for advantages only.

questions.stem: Which of the following BEST describes the terms 'stimulus' and 'response'?

options.A: Stimulus: A deliberate provocation of an organism | Response: The movement of an organism
options.B: Stimulus: An organism's exposure to sunlight | Response: The growth of an organism away from light
options.C: Stimulus: A wave of excitement in an organism | Response: Locomotion of an organism
options.D: Stimulus: A detectable change in an organism's environment | Response: An organism's reactions to a stimulus

questions.explanation:
The textbook definitions:

  • Stimulus — a detectable change in the environment.
  • Response — the organism's reaction to that stimulus.

Row D states both correctly.

distractors.A: Too narrow — provocation isn't the only stimulus, and movement isn't the only response.
distractors.B: Specific example, not a definition.
distractors.C: "Wave of excitement" isn't standard biological vocabulary.

questions.stem: The diagrams below show four shoots in light: I (foil cap on tip), II (tip removed), III (intact tip), IV (transparent cap on tip). After a few days, the TALLEST shoot would MOST likely be
questions.explanation: Tip-removed shoots (II) cannot grow at all — auxin comes from the tip. Shoots III and IV both detect light through the tip and bend toward the light (so vertical height is reduced). Shoot I has a foil cap blocking light from reaching the tip — no phototropism, so the shoot grows straight upward and reaches the greatest vertical height.

distractors.B: II has no tip = no growth at all. Shortest, not tallest.
distractors.C: III has its tip exposed and bends toward the light — height reduced.
distractors.D: IV has a transparent cap — light gets through, shoot bends. Shorter than I.

diagram.alt: Four shoots in light with various caps and tip conditions

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a reflex arc with structures labelled A (in skin), B (in spinal cord), C, and D. Which labelled structure relays the impulse to an effector?
questions.explanation: The motor neurone carries impulses from the spinal cord OUT to the effector (muscle). In this diagram, label C shows the motor neurone path from the spinal cord down to the muscle.

distractors.A: A is the receptor (in skin) — detects the stimulus.
distractors.B: B is the relay neurone (inside the spinal cord) — connects sensory and motor.
distractors.D: D is the sensory neurone — carries impulse FROM receptor TO spinal cord.

diagram.alt: Reflex arc diagram with four labelled structures

questions.stem: The function of the choroid layer in the eye is to

options.C: maintain the shape of the eyeball
options.D: control the amount of light entering the eye

questions.explanation: The choroid is a dark, pigmented layer between the sclera and the retina. Its melanin absorbs stray light and prevents it from reflecting back inside the eye, which would blur the image.

distractors.A: The cornea and lens focus light, not the choroid.
distractors.C: The sclera (white outer layer) maintains eyeball shape.
distractors.D: The iris controls light entry by adjusting pupil size.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a section of human skin with regions labelled I, II, III and IV. Which structure is NOT involved in temperature regulation?
questions.explanation: Temperature regulation in skin involves: sweat glands (sweating cools), hair erector muscles (raise hairs to trap air), and blood vessels (vasodilation/constriction). The sebaceous gland (label II) secretes oil (sebum) for skin and hair lubrication — it does NOT play a role in temperature regulation.

distractors.A: I (hair) is involved — hair erector muscles raise hairs to trap warm air.
distractors.C: III (sweat gland) is involved — sweating cools the body.
distractors.D: IV (blood vessels/capillaries) is involved — vasodilation/constriction adjusts heat loss.

diagram.alt: Section of human skin showing epidermis, dermis, four labelled structures, and cornified layer

questions.stem: The diagram below shows a sigmoid graph illustrating a measurement of growth in living organisms. Which of the following labels would be INCORRECT on the y-axis?
questions.explanation: Time is the independent variable on a growth curve — it goes on the x-axis. The y-axis shows the dependent variable: a measure of growth (mass, length, count).

distractors.A: Mass is a valid growth measure for the y-axis.
distractors.B: Length is a valid growth measure for the y-axis.
distractors.D: Number of leaves is a valid growth measure (especially in plants).

diagram.alt: Sigmoid (S-shaped) growth curve

questions.stem: The following statements describe the processes taking place within a seed during germination: I. Embryo uses food to develop the radicle and plumule. II. Enzymes break down proteins into amino acids. III. Soluble products move into the embryo. Which of the following sequences is in the correct order of the processes?
questions.explanation:
Germination order:

  1. II — Enzymes break down stored food (proteins → amino acids).
  2. III — Soluble products move from the cotyledon to the embryo.
  3. I — Embryo uses the food to develop the radicle and plumule.

distractors.A: Has the embryo using food before food has been broken down.
distractors.B: Same problem.
distractors.C: Has the embryo using food before soluble products have moved to it.

questions.stem: The diagrams below show two specialised cells (an egg/ovum and a sperm). Which of the following structures is common to both cells?
questions.explanation: Both gametes carry a nucleus containing genetic material — that is the whole point of fertilisation: combining the nuclei of egg and sperm. The other structures listed are sperm-specific.

distractors.A: Cilia are short hair-like structures, not typical of either gamete.
distractors.C: Flagellum (tail) is found only on the sperm.
distractors.D: The acrosome is found only on the sperm head.

diagram.alt: Egg and sperm specialised cells

questions.stem: Which of the following forms of birth control is MOST likely 100% effective?
questions.explanation: Tubal ligation surgically blocks the fallopian tubes, preventing eggs from reaching the uterus. Effectively permanent and over 99% effective — closer to 100% than any other option.

distractors.A: Condoms are about 85% effective with typical use.
distractors.B: Diaphragms are about 88% effective with typical use.
distractors.D: The pill is about 91% effective with typical use.

questions.stem: The diagrams below show flowers from two different types of plants (I — bright tubular petals; II — feathery stigmas, dangling stamens). Which of the following statements is true for the flowers?

options.A: I is pollinated by a hummingbird; II is pollinated by the wind.
options.C: Petals are absent from both I and II.

questions.explanation: Flower I has bright tubular petals — typical of bird-pollinated flowers. Flower II has feathery stigmas and dangling stamens — classic features of wind-pollinated flowers.

distractors.B: Flower II's features adapt for wind, not birds.
distractors.C: Flower I clearly has petals.
distractors.D: Flower I is showy and tubular — that's bird pollination.

diagram.alt: Two flowers — I bird-pollinated, II wind-pollinated

questions.stem: Which of the following is the correct sequence for seed formation after fertilization?
questions.explanation:
The ovule already exists before fertilisation. After fertilisation:

  1. Ovule contains the egg.
  2. Zygote forms when sperm and egg fuse.
  3. Embryo develops as the zygote divides.
  4. Seed matures.

distractors.B: Has zygote at the end — but the zygote forms BEFORE the embryo.
distractors.C: Embryo can't exist before zygote.
distractors.D: Embryo can't come before zygote.

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the life cycle of an organism (I = egg, II = larvae, III = pupa, IV = adult flying mosquito). At which stage is this organism most easily controlled by the use of aerosol insecticides?
questions.explanation: Aerosol insecticides are sprayed in the air. They are most effective against the adult flying stage (IV) — adult mosquitoes encounter the spray as they fly. The aquatic stages (II larvae, III pupa) are underwater, and eggs (I) are on the water surface — all harder to reach with aerosols.

distractors.A: Eggs are on the water surface — also poorly reached by aerosols.
distractors.B: Larvae live underwater — not exposed to aerosol sprays.
distractors.C: Pupae are also aquatic.

diagram.alt: Mosquito life cycle with four stages

questions.stem: The diagram below shows the stages of mitosis labelled I, II, III, IV. The correct order of the stages is
questions.explanation: Mitosis stages in order: prophase → metaphase → anaphase → telophase. From the typical CSEC diagram labelling: III (prophase, chromosomes condensed) → II (metaphase, chromosomes lined up at equator) → I (anaphase, chromatids pulled to poles) → IV (telophase, two daughter nuclei forming).

distractors.A: Starts with metaphase before prophase.
distractors.B: Wrong order between stages.
distractors.D: Starts with telophase — that's the END of mitosis, not the beginning.

diagram.alt: Four stages of mitosis labelled I, II, III, IV

questions.stem: Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of meiosis?
questions.explanation: Meiosis HALVES the chromosome number — diploid (2n) parent cells produce haploid (n) gametes. Maintenance of the diploid number is what mitosis does, NOT meiosis.

distractors.B: Genetic recombination IS a characteristic — it generates variation.
distractors.C: Crossing over IS a characteristic of meiosis (during prophase I).
distractors.D: Random assortment IS a characteristic — chromosomes line up randomly at metaphase I.

questions.stem: Which of the following terms refers to visible characteristics of living things?
questions.explanation: Phenotype = the observable, visible traits of an organism (eye colour, height, blood group, etc.). It results from the genotype interacting with the environment.

distractors.A: A gene is a section of DNA — invisible directly.
distractors.B: Genotype is the genetic make-up — also invisible directly.
distractors.D: A chromosome is the packaged form of DNA — invisible to the naked eye.

questions.stem: One example of a sex-linked disease is
questions.explanation: Colour blindness is X-linked recessive — the gene is on the X chromosome. Males (XY) only need one copy to be affected; females (XX) need two. This is why colour blindness is more common in males.

distractors.A: Cancer involves many genes and environmental factors, not specifically sex chromosomes.
distractors.B: Hypertension is influenced by lifestyle and many genes — not a single sex-linked gene.
distractors.D: Down syndrome is caused by an extra chromosome 21 (autosomal), not a sex-linked gene.

questions.stem: A species consists of members that
questions.explanation: A species is defined as a group of organisms that can interbreed under natural conditions to produce fertile offspring. The biological species concept.

distractors.A: Many different species occupy the same habitat — habitat alone doesn't define species.
distractors.B: Asexual reproduction defines a single individual's reproductive mode, not a species.
distractors.D: Producing infertile offspring (e.g., mules from horse × donkey) means the parents are different species, not the same.

questions.stem: Which of the following would be involved in the production of insulin by genetic engineering? I. Transfer of the human insulin gene to a bacterium II. A vector to transfer the insulin gene III. Production of insulin by the bacterium
questions.explanation:
All three steps are involved in producing insulin by genetic engineering:

  • I — the human insulin gene is transferred to a bacterium.
  • II — a vector (typically a plasmid) is used to ferry the gene into the bacterium.
  • III — the modified bacterium then expresses the gene and produces insulin.

distractors.A: Excludes the vector and production steps.
distractors.B: Excludes the production step.
distractors.C: Excludes the gene-transfer step.

questions.stem: Which of the following statements about recycling manufactured materials are true? I. Less energy is used in the manufacturing of new products by using the raw materials from recycled products. II. Some manufactured products can be recycled. III. Recycling of some non-biodegradable materials prevents landfill sites from filling up too quickly and for a long time.
questions.explanation: All three statements about recycling are correct. I — recycling raw materials saves the energy that would have been spent extracting and refining new ones. II — most manufactured products can be reprocessed (paper, glass, metals, many plastics). III — every non-biodegradable item that goes to a recycling plant is one less item filling up a landfill, slowing the rate at which landfills fill up.

distractors.A: I and II are true but III is also a major reason societies recycle non-biodegradable items — leaving III out misses the landfill argument.
distractors.B: Skipping II ignores that very many manufactured products are recyclable, which is the whole reason recycling programmes work.
distractors.C: Skipping I leaves out the energy-saving benefit, which is the most-cited reason for industrial-scale recycling.

questions.stem: When compared to a cheek cell, a muscle cell contains more
questions.explanation: Muscle cells are highly active and need to release a great deal of ATP to power contraction. They therefore contain far more mitochondria than a typical cheek (epithelial) cell, which is relatively low-energy.

distractors.A: Vacuoles are mainly a feature of plant cells; muscle and cheek cells both have only small temporary vacuoles.
distractors.B: Both cells need ribosomes to make proteins, but muscle is not especially ribosome-rich compared with cheek cells.
distractors.D: Chromosome number is determined by species, not cell type — both human muscle and cheek cells have the same 46 chromosomes.

questions.stem: Which of the labelled structures is responsible for controlling cellular activities?
questions.explanation: The structure labelled IV is the nucleus. The nucleus contains the cell's DNA — the genetic blueprint that codes for every protein the cell makes — so it controls all cellular activities.

distractors.A: I is the cell wall — provides rigid support for plant cells, not control of activities.
distractors.B: II is a chloroplast — the site of photosynthesis, not control of cellular activities.
distractors.C: III is the large central vacuole — stores cell sap and helps maintain turgor pressure.

diagram.alt: Plant cell diagram with parts I, II, III and IV labelled

questions.stem: Which of the following functions is NOT correctly matched to its organelle?
questions.explanation: Protein synthesis is carried out by ribosomes, NOT chloroplasts. Chloroplasts are the site of photosynthesis. So option B is the wrongly-matched pair.

distractors.A: The cell membrane does control osmotic balance — osmosis happens across it. Correctly matched.
distractors.C: The nucleus does contain DNA (hereditary material). Correctly matched.
distractors.D: Mitochondria are the powerhouses of the cell — they release energy from glucose. Correctly matched.

questions.stem: The food produced in the photosynthesis process is
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis converts CO₂ and water (using light energy + chlorophyll) into glucose, with oxygen as a by-product. Glucose is the immediate food product. The plant later converts some glucose into starch for storage, into amino acids/proteins (after combining with nitrates), or into fats — but the direct product of photosynthesis is glucose.

distractors.A: Fats are made later from glucose — they are not the direct product.
distractors.C: Proteins are built from amino acids — also a downstream product, not the direct food made.
distractors.D: Amino acids require nitrogen (from soil nitrates) plus glucose — they are not the direct photosynthetic product.

questions.stem: Which of the following organisms is a saprophyte?
questions.explanation: A saprophyte is an organism that feeds on dead and decaying organic matter by secreting enzymes and absorbing the digested products. Bread mould (a fungus) is a classic example — it grows on dead bread and decomposes it.

distractors.B: A mango tree is a producer (autotroph) — it makes its own food via photosynthesis.
distractors.C: Amoeba is a holozoic feeder — it engulfs food particles, not a saprophyte.
distractors.D: A cat is a carnivore (holozoic feeder) — eats living/recently-killed prey, not decaying matter.

questions.stem: The BEST description of the role of excretion in living organisms is to
questions.explanation: Excretion is defined as the removal of toxic metabolic waste produced by the cells (e.g., urea, CO₂, excess salts). Without excretion these wastes would accumulate to toxic levels and kill the cell.

distractors.A: Producing urine is one method of excretion — it's the means, not the role itself. Toxic waste removal is the underlying purpose.
distractors.B: Producing faeces is egestion, not excretion — faeces is undigested food, not metabolic waste.
distractors.D: Removing excess water is part of excretion (via urine and sweat) but is only one component — the main role is removing toxic waste.

questions.stem: In humans, the skeletal system performs a variety of functions including
questions.explanation: The human skeleton has multiple functions — blood cell formation (haematopoiesis) happens in the red bone marrow inside long bones (femur, sternum, pelvis). The skeleton also gives support, allows movement, protects organs, and stores minerals (Ca, P).

distractors.B: Hormones are made by endocrine glands (pituitary, thyroid, adrenals, pancreas), not by bones.
distractors.C: The immune response is carried out by white blood cells (lymphocytes etc.); the skeleton only makes them, it doesn't carry out the immune response itself.
distractors.D: Mastication (chewing) is performed by the muscles of the jaw and the teeth, not by the skeletal system as a whole.

questions.stem: Tarzan and Jane are heterozygous for blood groups A and B respectively. Which of the following shows all of the possible results of their genetic cross?
questions.explanation:
Tarzan is heterozygous group A → genotype IAi (alleles A and O). Jane is heterozygous group B → genotype IBi. Cross:

  • IA + IB = IAIB → blood group AB (25%)
  • IA + i = IAi → blood group A (25%)
  • i + IB = IBi → blood group B (25%)
  • i + i = ii → blood group O (25%)

So 25% A : 25% B : 25% AB : 25% O — option D.

distractors.A: 100% AB would only happen if both parents were homozygous A and B respectively (I<sup>A</sup>I<sup>A</sup> × I<sup>B</sup>I<sup>B</sup>). They are heterozygous, so this is impossible.
distractors.B: Misses group B and group O entirely — doesn't account for the recessive O allele each parent carries.
distractors.C: Misses group A entirely — every cross between A and B heterozygotes must produce all four blood groups in equal ratio.

questions.stem: Which of the following relationships may present harm to one of the organisms involved? I. Commensalism  II. Parasitism  III. Predation
questions.explanation:
Parasitism harms the host (the parasite benefits while the host is harmed). Predation kills the prey (the predator benefits while the prey is harmed/killed). So both II and III involve harm to one organism.

Commensalism (I) is a relationship where one benefits and the other is unaffected — neither is harmed.

distractors.A: I (commensalism) does not harm either organism.
distractors.B: Same problem — commensalism is harmless.
distractors.D: Commensalism is harmless to both, so it cannot be included.

questions.stem: Human activities would have the LEAST immediate impact on
questions.explanation: Fossil fuels form over millions of years from buried prehistoric organic matter. Human activity has essentially no immediate effect on this process — it takes geological time. (Of course, burning the existing fossil fuels DOES have immediate effects, but creating them does not.)

distractors.B: Coral bleaching happens within weeks of warm-water spikes — humans drive ocean warming through emissions, an immediate impact.
distractors.C: Overpopulation is directly driven by human reproductive choices — immediate.
distractors.D: Eutrophication happens within seasons of fertiliser/sewage runoff — immediate human impact.

questions.stem: The samples X, Y, and Z respectively MOST likely contain — given X gives violet colour with sodium hydroxide and copper sulphate; Y gives blue/black precipitate with iodine; Z gives orange precipitate when heated with Benedict's reagent after hydrolysis.
questions.explanation:
Match each test to its food group:

  • Biuret test (NaOH + CuSO₄) → violet means protein. So X = protein.
  • Iodine → blue-black means starch. So Y = starch.
  • Benedict's after hydrolysis → orange precipitate. The hydrolysis converts a non-reducing sugar (e.g., sucrose) into reducing sugars (glucose + fructose), which then react with Benedict's. So Z = non-reducing sugar.

distractors.A: Reverses X and Z — Biuret violet is protein not non-reducing sugar.
distractors.B: Wrong assignment for Y — iodine doesn't indicate sugar.
distractors.C: Wrong assignment for X — iodine indicates starch (this is Y, not X).

questions.stem: Persons who suffer from kidney disease are MOST likely to have

options.A: a decreased concentration of glucose in the blood
options.B: an increased concentration of protein in the blood
options.C: a decreased concentration of urea in the blood
options.D: an increased concentration of urea in the blood

questions.explanation: The kidneys remove urea from the blood (a toxic nitrogenous waste from protein breakdown). When kidneys fail, urea cannot be excreted and accumulates in the blood — a condition called uraemia.

distractors.A: Glucose levels are mainly controlled by the pancreas (insulin/glucagon), not the kidneys.
distractors.B: Healthy kidneys retain protein in the blood; failing kidneys may LET MORE protein leak into urine, REDUCING blood protein — not increasing it.
distractors.C: The opposite of what happens — failing kidneys lead to MORE urea in blood, not less.
